title: MetaInfer: A Knowledge Only LLM Inference Engine Generator SKILL Toolbox
summary: As LLM technology advances, the space of model families, compute hardware, quantization schemes, parallelization strategies, and specialized optimization kernels continues to expand, sharply increasing the code complexity and maintenance cost of general-purpose inference frameworks. Conventional software engineering uses multiple layers of abstraction to support diverse application scenarios, but these abstractions also increase system complexity and may introduce additional performance overhead. This paper presents metainfer, an 'LLM-as-Compiler' approach in which users specify only the runtime constraints of an inference program. An LLM-driven multi-agent collaboration system, coupled with a contract knowledge base, then automatically generates a compact customized inference framework that satisfies these constraints. We evaluate metainfer from three perspectives: the effect of source-code reference, the runtime behavior and performance profile of engines generated under the zero-reference constraint on CKB-covered targets, and knowledge-base evolution for new model and platform scenarios. The results show that metainfer organizes generation constraints, validation feedback, and knowledge consolidation into a continuous closed loop, enabling runnable customized inference solutions to be generated from explicit knowledge. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/MetaInfer/MetaInfer.

AI.tldr: MetaInfer is an LLM-as-compiler system that automatically generates customized inference frameworks from runtime constraints, reducing complexity while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: The rapid expansion of LLM model families, hardware, quantization schemes, and optimization strategies has dramatically increased code complexity and maintenance costs in general-purpose inference frameworks. Traditional abstraction layers add system complexity and performance overhead.
AI.method: MetaInfer uses an LLM-driven multi-agent collaboration system with a contract knowledge base. Users specify only runtime constraints, and the system automatically generates compact customized inference frameworks that satisfy these constraints.
AI.result: The system successfully organizes generation constraints, validation feedback, and knowledge consolidation into a continuous closed loop. It can generate runnable customized inference solutions from explicit knowledge and works under zero-reference constraints on covered targets.
AI.conclusion: MetaInfer demonstrates that LLM-as-compiler approaches can effectively address the complexity problem in inference frameworks by automating customized framework generation while maintaining performance through explicit knowledge representation and continuous learning.

title: Ontology-Amplified Distillation and Contextuality Auditing for Sovereign Enterprise Language Models: A Combined Proof-of-Mechanism and Negative-Results Method Study
comment: 15 pages, 2 figures. Combined proof-of-mechanism and negative-results method article consolidating ontology-amplified distillation with contextuality-audit routing for enterprise agents
summary: Regulated financial institutions operating under data-residency rules need tenant-owned language models that can run inside the institution's perimeter. This paper combines two related FAOS studies into one mechanism-and-control article. First, it reports a reduced-power proof-of-mechanism study of ontology-amplified distillation: a Qwen3.6-27B student is adapted to the Foundation AgenticOS ontology through supervised fine-tuning on frontier-teacher trajectories and ontology-grounded direct preference optimization (DPO), trained locally on a single Apple M5 Max from 47 synthetic, English-language, cross-domain preference pairs. On 40 held-out Vietnamese financial-domain tasks, the distilled student grounds 36 of 40 tasks (grounded rate 0.90; mean ontology term-coverage r_onto = 0.95 on a metric floored at 0.50), equal to the GPT-5 frontier baseline, which also grounds 36 of 40. The outcome is underpowered to establish equivalence: the paired-difference 95% confidence interval spans +/-4 tasks, and the run does not test or show the pre-registered amplification prediction that the student should exceed the frontier. Second, the paper consolidates a contextuality-audit method for enterprise-agent routing. In a separate negative-results pilot, the corrected canonical Contextuality-by-Default degree is zero for all Phase 1.3 groups in both the local-Qwen run and an explicitly labeled Gemma replication check; the useful signal is direct influence and construct coupling, not surviving residual contextuality. Together, the studies pair an ontology-grounded model-building mechanism with a governance diagnostic for deciding when apparent disagreement should trigger prompt standardization, multi-agent synthesis, or human review. The evidence supports neither deployability, safety, superiority, statistical equivalence, nor a contextuality-positive routing rule.

AI.tldr: The paper combines two studies: (1) testing ontology-amplified distillation for local LLM training on financial data with data residency requirements, achieving results comparable to GPT-5 but underpowered for equivalence claims; (2) developing a contextuality audit for enterprise agent routing, finding no significant contextuality effects.
AI.motivation: Regulated financial institutions need LLMs that can run inside their security perimeters due to data-residency rules, requiring locally trainable models. Additionally, enterprise AI systems need governance diagnostics to manage disagreements among agents.
AI.method: Combines two methods: (1) Ontology-amplified distillation using supervised fine-tuning on frontier-teacher trajectories and ontology-grounded DPO, trained locally on a single Apple M5 Max with synthetic English preference pairs; (2) Contextuality audit method for enterprise-agent routing using Contextuality-by-Default analysis.
AI.result: First study: Distilled Qwen3.6-27B achieved 90% grounding rate on Vietnamese financial tasks, equal to GPT-5 baseline, but study was underpowered to establish equivalence. Second study: Found zero contextuality in Phase 1.3 groups for both test and replication, suggesting useful signal comes from direct influence/construct coupling rather than residual contextuality.
AI.conclusion: The evidence doesn't support claims of deployability, safety, superiority, statistical equivalence, or contextuality-positive routing rules. However, it pairs ontology-grounded model-building with governance diagnostics for deciding when disagreements should trigger standardization, multi-agent synthesis, or human review.

title: Graph Feedback Controls Consensus and Clique Formation in Open-Weight Language-Model Populations
summary: Multi-agent language-model systems increasingly route local interactions, yet the runtime interaction graph is often treated as an implementation detail. We study convention formation in open-weight LM populations spanning 1.1B-32B parameters with a naming-game protocol. Restricted first-token scores over tokenizer-safe labels let us measure prompt-conditioned score-state distributions, construct state-similarity graphs, and separate sampled-label agreement from latent state-space consensus. Across controlled interventions, in the main open-weight repair grids, retained partner-label evidence is necessary but not sufficient: homophilous threshold-similarity routing deletes cross-basin exposure and amplifies fragmentation, while bridge-seeking routing often repairs fragmentation when memory is available. In a three-seed mixed four-model grid, threshold-similarity produces no final behavioral or state consensus in 189 setting-seed runs, whereas state-component and label-disagreement bridges recover final behavioral consensus in 14/18 retained-memory runs. Across homogeneous model populations, retained history generally shifts fragmented dynamics toward consensus; the clearest case is Qwen2.5-32B, which reaches stable behavioral and final state consensus in all 18 retained-history well-mixed settings, while threshold-similarity reaches neither form of consensus in 189 settings. Robustness over state thresholds, population size, and vocabulary size preserves the qualitative ordering, and early-window graph-energy features provide useful within-grid diagnostics.

AI.tldr: Study shows that convention formation in open-weight LM populations depends on interaction routing strategies, where threshold-similarity routing amplifies fragmentation while bridge-seeking routing with memory enables consensus.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent language-model systems increasingly manage local interactions, but their runtime interaction graphs are often overlooked despite being crucial for understanding how conventions emerge in open-weight LM populations.
AI.method: Used naming-game protocol across LM populations (1.1B-32B parameters); measured prompt-conditioned score-state distributions via restricted first-token scores; constructed state-similarity graphs; separated sampled-label agreement from latent state-space consensus; tested controlled interventions including threshold-similarity routing vs. bridge-seeking routing.
AI.result: Threshold-similarity routing amplifies fragmentation and prevents consensus, while bridge-seeking routing with memory enables consensus formation; Qwen2.5-32B with retained history reached stable behavioral and state consensus in all settings, while threshold-similarity failed in 189 settings.
AI.conclusion: Interaction routing strategy critically determines convention formation in open-weight LM populations—homophilous threshold-similarity routing hinders consensus while bridge-seeking routing with memory facilitates it, highlighting the importance of considering interaction graphs in multi-agent LM system design.

title: PFAdapter: Hierarchical LoRA Decomposition for Personalized Federated MLLMs
summary: Agentic AI systems are reshaping communications and networking by deploying autonomous intelligent agents capable of collaborative learning while maintaining data privacy at network edges. Within distributed network environments, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) serve as cognitive engines for edge devices, yet federated fine-tuning faces substantial challenges in balancing global knowledge aggregation with local adaptation under heterogeneous network conditions. Conventional federated protocols typically rely on uniform parameter aggregation, which conflates domain-invariant features with client-specific nuances, thereby resulting in suboptimal personalization and excessive communication overhead. To address these challenges, we propose PFAdapter, a communication-efficient framework introducing hierarchical LoRA decomposition to explicitly separate adapter parameters into global-shared and local-private components. Query and key projections are assigned to global synchronization for capturing universal multimodal semantics across the network, while value and output projections remain localized for edge-specific adaptation. Additionally, orthogonality regularization based on the Frobenius norm enforces strict separation between these components, preventing redundant feature learning. Selective aggregation protocols synchronize only global-shared components across the federated network, preserving local expertise and reducing communication costs by nearly 50%. Extensive experiments on VQA-RAD, SLAKE, Hateful Memes, and CrisisMMD datasets demonstrate that PFAdapter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving accuracy improvements ranging from 2.4% to 4.8% across diverse edge intelligence tasks. Consequently, our framework establishes an efficient solution for agentic AI deployment in resource-constrained communication networks.

AI.tldr: PFAdapter is a communication-efficient federated learning framework that uses hierarchical LoRA decomposition to separate global-shared and local-private adapter parameters for agentic AI systems, reducing communication overhead by 50% while improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Federated fine-tuning of MLLMs at network edges faces challenges in balancing global knowledge aggregation with local adaptation under heterogeneous conditions. Conventional federated protocols use uniform parameter aggregation that conflates domain-invariant features with client-specific nuances, leading to suboptimal personalization and excessive communication overhead.
AI.method: PFAdapter introduces hierarchical LoRA decomposition to explicitly separate adapter parameters into global-shared (query/key projections) and local-private (value/output projections) components. It uses orthogonality regularization based on the Frobenius norm to enforce strict separation and selective aggregation protocols that synchronize only global components across the federated network.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on VQA-RAD, SLAKE, Hateful Memes, and CrisisMMD datasets show PFAdapter consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving accuracy improvements of 2.4% to 4.8% across diverse edge intelligence tasks while reducing communication costs by nearly 50%.
AI.conclusion: PFAdapter establishes an efficient solution for agentic AI deployment in resource-constrained communication networks by effectively separating global knowledge aggregation from local adaptation, enabling better personalization while minimizing communication overhead.

title: OmniPMNet: Bridging discrete and gridded PM10 forecasts via omni-query neural processes
summary: Forecasting particulate matter (PM10) requires both station-scale accuracy and continuous spatial fields, especially during severe dust storms. Chemical transport models (CTMs) provide gridded forecasts but retain local biases, whereas graph neural networks (GNNs) track monitoring sites well at short lead times but do not produce gridded outputs. Here we present OmniPM-Net, a Convolutional Conditional Neural Process (ConvCNP)-based fusion model that reconciles these two forecast types within a shared spatial representation. A terrain-aware Gaussian set convolution lifts irregular GNN station forecasts onto a regular grid, where a multi-scale Spatial Source Attention (SSA) module blends them with Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) forecasts; a shared omni-query readout then decodes this representation into consistent PM10 predictions at either stations or grid cells over a 108 h horizon. Evaluated across 1,618 air-quality monitoring stations throughout China over the full year of 2024, OmniPM-Net matches the station-level accuracy of the stronger GNN baseline (mean absolute error 21.14 versus 22.00 ug/m3) and reduces the CAMS mean absolute error by 30%, while simultaneously delivering the gridded fields that the discrete GNN cannot. Its clearest gains are in the high-concentration tail, where the 90th-percentile MAE falls by 9% relative to the GNN and 25% relative to CAMS, and during dust episodes, where it improves categorical detection skill while tracking the evolving spatial trajectory.

AI.tldr: OmniPM-Net is a hybrid model that combines station-level GNN forecasts with gridded CTM forecasts using convolutional conditional neural processes to produce accurate PM10 predictions at both monitoring stations and grid cells over 108 hours.
AI.motivation: Current PM10 forecasting approaches have complementary limitations: chemical transport models provide gridded forecasts but have local biases, while graph neural networks offer accurate station-level short-term forecasts but lack spatial coverage. There's a need for models that can provide both station-scale accuracy and continuous spatial fields, especially during severe dust storms.
AI.method: Uses a Convolutional Conditional Neural Process framework with three key components: 1) terrain-aware Gaussian set convolution to lift irregular GNN station forecasts onto a regular grid, 2) multi-scale Spatial Source Attention module to blend station forecasts with CAMS gridded forecasts, and 3) shared omni-query readout to decode representations into consistent PM10 predictions at both stations and grid cells over 108 hours.
AI.result: Evaluated across 1,618 stations in China throughout 2024, OmniPM-Net matches station-level accuracy of GNN baseline (MAE 21.14 vs 22.00 μg/m³) while reducing CAMS MAE by 30%. Most significant improvements in high-concentration tail (90th-percentile MAE reduced by 9% vs GNN, 25% vs CAMS) and during dust episodes with better categorical detection skill and spatial trajectory tracking.
AI.conclusion: OmniPM-Net successfully reconciles the strengths of GNNs and CTMs, providing accurate PM10 forecasts at both station and grid scales with particular benefits for extreme pollution events and dust storms, enabling better air quality management.

title: Optimal Adaptive Market Making: A Theoretical Framework for High-Yield Liquidity Provision in Perpetual Futures Markets
summary: We develop a rigorous theoretical framework for optimal market making in perpetual futures markets with zero maker fees. We model the market maker's problem as a stochastic optimal control problem on a filtered probability space, where the controls are adaptive bid-ask spreads and inventory hedging decisions across two exchanges. Our contributions include: (i) a PnL decomposition theorem separating revenue into spread income, adverse selection loss, inventory carrying cost, hedging friction, and funding rate exposure; (ii) the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation for the joint spread-inventory-hedging control problem under CARA utility with a verification theorem; (iii) High-APY Regime Theorems characterizing profitable regions via five dimensionless parameters, culminating in a Master APY Formula; (iv) analysis of zero-fee economics on decentralized perpetual exchanges with optimal entry-exit thresholds; (v) optimal cross-exchange hedging policies with funding rate dynamics and a hedge regime trichotomy; (vi) a robustness margin quantifying parameter uncertainty tolerance; (vii) exponential drawdown probability bounds and a universal APY-VaR identity; (viii) ergodic inventory distribution under optimal control with Bayesian adaptive estimation; (ix) Kelly-optimal leverage with ruin boundaries; and (x) multi-pair portfolio allocation with diversification saturation results. Numerical analysis with twenty-three figures reveals phase transitions between profitable and unprofitable regimes. Our framework unifies and extends the Avellaneda-Stoikov, Gueant-Lehalle-Fernandez-Tapia, and Glosten-Milgrom paradigms for modern decentralized venue microstructure.

AI.tldr: Theoretical framework for optimal market making in zero-fee perpetual futures markets with cross-exchange hedging, PnL decomposition, and regime characterization for profitability.
AI.motivation: To develop a rigorous theoretical framework for market making in modern decentralized perpetual futures markets, which operate with zero maker fees, requiring new models beyond classical approaches.
AI.method: Stochastic optimal control problem on filtered probability space with adaptive bid-ask spreads and inventory hedging across exchanges, using CARA utility with HJB equation and verification theorem, plus numerical analysis with 23 figures.
AI.result: Developed comprehensive framework with PnL decomposition theorem, High-APY Regime Theorems, optimal entry-exit thresholds, cross-exchange hedging policies, robustness margins, APY-VaR identity, ergodic inventory distribution, Kelly-optimal leverage, and multi-pair portfolio allocation results.
AI.conclusion: The framework unifies and extends classical market making paradigms (Avellaneda-Stoikov, Gueant-Lehalle-Fernandez-Tapia, Glosten-Milgrom) for modern decentralized perpetual venues, revealing phase transitions between profitable/unprofitable regimes with theoretical guarantees.

title: Internet of Agentic Things: Networked AI Agents for Closed-Loop IoT Orchestration
summary: The paper introduces the Internet of Agentic Things (IoAT), an architectural framework that integrates agentic AI, IoT, cyber-physical systems, Physical AI, edge computing, and digital twins into a unified closed-loop orchestration framework. The proposed architecture consists of cloud, edge/fog, and physical IoT layers connected through autonomous AI agents that perceive, reason, coordinate, and actuate across distributed cyber-physical environments. The paper formalizes IoAT as a coupled workflow-control problem with nested strategic and tactical decision making using a hylomorphic dynamic programming framework that links agentic planning with physical execution. Smart-building orchestration is presented as a representative use case, and key research challenges related to safety, security, governance, resilience, and trustworthy deployment are discussed.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Internet of Agentic Things (IoAT), an architectural framework that integrates agentic AI, IoT, cyber-physical systems, Physical AI, edge computing, and digital twins into a unified closed-loop orchestration system with autonomous AI agents coordinating across cloud, edge/fog, and physical IoT layers.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for a unified framework to integrate increasingly complex and autonomous AI systems with physical IoT environments, enabling more sophisticated closed-loop orchestration across distributed cyber-physical systems rather than having isolated solutions.
AI.method: The authors propose a three-layer architecture (cloud, edge/fog, and physical IoT) connected through autonomous AI agents with perception, reasoning, coordination, and actuation capabilities. They formalize IoAT as a coupled workflow-control problem using a hylomorphic dynamic programming framework that links agentic planning with physical execution, featuring nested strategic and tactical decision making.
AI.result: The paper presents the IoAT architectural framework and demonstrates its application through a smart-building orchestration use case. The framework enables coordinated, closed-loop control across distributed cyber-physical environments with autonomous AI agents managing the orchestration.
AI.conclusion: The IoAT framework provides a promising approach for integrating agentic AI with IoT and cyber-physical systems, but significant research challenges remain in areas of safety, security, governance, resilience, and trustworthy deployment that need to be addressed for real-world implementation.

title: Semidirect Fourier Delta Attention: Phase-Controlled Delta Memory with Constructive Chunk-WY Kernels
summary: Linear attention replaces softmax attention's growing KV cache with a fixed recurrent state, but this compression limits exact state tracking and long-context memory. We introduce \emph{Semidirect Fourier Delta Attention} (SFDA), a phase-controlled generalization of Kimi Delta Attention that replaces real diagonal decay with block-rotational Fourier control: \[ S_t=(I-β_t k_tk_t^*)Λ_tS_{t-1}+β_tk_tv_t^*, \qquad Λ_t=\diag(α_t\odot e^{iθ_t}). \] Our main result is a constructive chunk-WY factorization for products \(A_t=Λ_t-u_tr_t^*\), giving \[ A_t\cdots A_1=Γ_t-Y_tM_tW_t^* \] with rank growth bounded inside fixed chunks. This yields an exact affine chunk transfer, formal stability and complexity bounds, and a compact characterization of phase-plus-low-rank memory. We verify the algebra numerically and show in toy state-tracking experiments that SFDA learns cyclic memory where the phase-disabled KDA baseline remains near chance. Fused kernels and large-scale language-model comparisons are left to future work.

AI.tldr: SFDA extends linear attention with phase-controlled Fourier rotations for better long-context memory, using chunk-WY factorization to bound rank growth and enable exact state tracking.
AI.motivation: Linear attention's fixed recurrent state compression limits exact state tracking and long-context memory capabilities, while standard approaches like Kimi Delta Attention use real diagonal decay which may not capture complex memory patterns.
AI.method: Introduces Semidirect Fourier Delta Attention (SFDA) with phase-controlled block-rotational Fourier control instead of real diagonal decay. Uses constructive chunk-WY factorization for products of matrices to achieve exact affine chunk transfer with bounded rank growth within fixed chunks.
AI.result: Developed mathematical formulation with formal stability and complexity bounds, compact characterization of phase-plus-low-rank memory. Verified algebra numerically and showed in toy experiments that SFDA learns cyclic memory while phase-disabled baselines perform near chance.
AI.conclusion: SFDA provides a mathematically rigorous framework for enhanced linear attention with phase control and bounded rank growth, offering improved memory capabilities, though large-scale language model comparisons remain for future work.

title: In-Context Reinforcement Learning under Non-Stationarity: A Survey
summary: The development of decision-pretrained transformers, algorithm distillation, long-context meta-RL, and retrieval-augmented agents has renewed interest in in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL): the ability of a pretrained or fine-tuned decision model to infer latent task rules and improve future behavior from interaction context, without test-time parameter updates. This line of work asks when trial-and-error evidence, rewards, transitions, demonstrations, feedback, or retrieved experience can make learning-like computation happen inside the context window. However, existing surveys of ICRL mainly organize the field around pretraining objectives, architectures, context formats, evaluation protocols, and theoretical mechanisms, while the non-stationary setting remains comparatively underexamined. In changing environments, accumulated context is not merely more evidence about a fixed task: the reward specification, transition kernel, observation channel, action interface, constraint model, or demonstration and memory distribution can fall out of alignment with the current regime. Previously useful context can therefore become stale, misleading, or useful again when an old regime returns. We survey non-stationary ICRL as the problem of adapting through context while deployed policy parameters remain fixed: the policy must infer both the current decision rule and which parts of its accumulated evidence still support that rule. We define non-stationary ICRL, relate it to meta-RL, decision sequence modeling, retrieval-augmented RL, value- and model-aware ICRL, and reward-feedback agents, and organize the literature along three questions: what changes, how the change unfolds, and how observable the change is to the agent.

AI.tldr: A survey paper examining non-stationary in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL), where agents must adapt to changing environments using only context information while keeping policy parameters fixed, analyzing what changes, how changes unfold, and how observable changes are to agents.
AI.motivation: Recent advances in decision-pretrained transformers and related methods have revived interest in ICRL, but existing surveys focus on pretraining objectives, architectures, and evaluation protocols while overlooking the critical challenge of non-stationary environments where context becomes stale or misleading as task rules change.
AI.method: The paper conducts a comprehensive survey of non-stationary ICRL, defining the problem as adapting through context with fixed policy parameters, relating it to meta-RL, decision sequence modeling, retrieval-augmented RL, and organizing literature around three key questions: what changes in environments, how changes unfold, and how observable changes are to agents.
AI.result: The survey provides a framework for understanding non-stationary ICRL by categorizing literature according to what components change (reward specification, transition kernel, observation channel, action interface, constraints, or demonstration distributions), how changes unfold (gradually, abruptly, periodically, or recurrently), and how observable changes are (fully, partially, or unobservable).
AI.conclusion: Non-stationary ICRL represents a critical frontier where agents must infer both current decision rules and which parts of accumulated evidence remain relevant, requiring new approaches that handle changing environments while maintaining fixed policy parameters through context adaptation rather than retraining.

title: Who Grades the Grader? Co-Evolving Evaluation Metrics and Skills for Self-Improving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving agent systems improve by creating, revising, and retiring their own skills, but every such loop rests on a hidden assumption: a reliable evaluation metric already exists. In many real applications it does not. We make three claims. First, metrics can be \emph{evolved}: our metric loop searches compositions of small drawback detectors under a full evolutionary lifecycle, trained to agree with a ten-item anchored reference set, regularized by consensus over unlabeled outputs, and audited against a held-out anchor it never reads, yielding a transparent, inspectable metric rather than an opaque judge. Second, since no metric exists to beat, the yardstick is recovering what an accurate metric would have enabled, and \emph{Double Ratchet}, our co-evolution of the metric with a lifecycle-managed skill loop, does so: across code generation (MBPP+), enterprise text-to-SQL (Spider~2.0-Snow), and reference-free report generation, it retains 88--110\% of the held-out lift achieved by the same skill loop driven by ground truth or the best available rubric. Third, safety comes from anchor discipline plus outer audits: removing anchor guards collapses the metric into a vacuous detector while removing the lifecycle does not; and when evolved skills gamed the report rubric, an independent judge caught it, one detector repaired it, and a task-aware judge then preferred the evolved outputs over the pre-evolution baseline in 77\% of decided pairs. We argue this failure-expecting architecture is the right default wherever no reliable automatic verifier exists.

AI.tldr: A novel "Double Ratchet" framework that co-evolves evaluation metrics alongside AI skills when no reliable automatic verifiers exist, enabling self-improvement systems in domains without ground truth metrics.
AI.motivation: Current self-evolving agent systems assume reliable evaluation metrics exist, but in many real applications they don't. The paper addresses this fundamental gap by proposing to evolve metrics alongside skills.
AI.method: Double Ratchet: a co-evolution framework where 1) a metric loop evolves compositions of small drawback detectors trained on a small anchored reference set, and 2) a skill loop evolves abilities, both under full evolutionary lifecycles with consensus regularization and external audits.
AI.result: Across code generation (MBPP+), enterprise text-to-SQL (Spider~2.0-Snow), and reference-free report generation, Double Ratchet retains 88-110% of the held-out lift achieved by using ground truth or best available rubrics. The system also demonstrates safety properties through anchor discipline and external audits.
AI.conclusion: The failure-expecting Double Ratchet architecture is the right default approach for domains lacking reliable automatic verifiers, as it enables meaningful self-improvement while maintaining safety through disciplined anchoring and external audits.

title: Repairing Shape-Prior Shortcuts in Long-Range Single-Shot Fringe Projection Profilometry
comment: 23 pages, 8 figures. Part 2 of a two-part study. Part 1 (diagnosis): arXiv:2606.17093
summary: Single-shot fringe projection profilometry (FPP) networks that regress depth directly can exploit a shape-prior shortcut, recovering depth from object boundaries rather than from fringe phase. On a photorealistic synthetic benchmark (15,600 fringe images, 50 objects at 1.5-2.1 m standoff), the best such UNet baseline plateaus at 14.54 mm object mean absolute error (MAE), and neither more data nor more capacity removes the shortcut, because neither changes the hypothesis space the optimizer searches. We introduce PhiCalNet, which outputs a wrapped-phase representation $(\sinφ, \cosφ)$ and maps it to depth through a fixed differentiable calibration layer, removing the shape-prior solution architecturally rather than by a loss penalty. Because the single-shot mapping is non-injective without fringe order, PhiCalNet takes the fringe order as auxiliary input, an assumption a sensitivity analysis shows tolerates realistic decoding error; a physics-informed (PINN) baseline with the same physics as a soft penalty yields no gain, isolating the architectural choice as the operative factor. PhiCalNet reduces object MAE 3.3x to 4.46 mm, its residual confined to 0.103% of pixels at the $\pmπ$ wrap discontinuity, and a three-frame extension reaches 1.16 mm. Two checks agree: interpretability makes phase the most decodable internal feature, and pixel-wise conformal uncertainty quantification, to our knowledge the first for FPP, localizes error at the same discontinuity, where rejecting the top 5% of pixels by snapshot disagreement cuts root-mean-square error by 64% versus 3.5% for the baseline.

AI.tldr: PhiCalNet introduces a novel fringe projection profilometry network that outputs wrapped-phase representation instead of directly regressing depth, eliminating shape-prior shortcuts and achieving 3.3x better accuracy than UNet baselines.
AI.motivation: Single-shot FPP networks that directly regress depth exploit a shape-prior shortcut, recovering depth from object boundaries rather than fringe phase, limiting accuracy. Current methods plateau at 14.54 mm MAE and neither more data nor capacity solves this fundamental optimization issue.
AI.method: PhiCalNet outputs wrapped-phase representation (sinφ, cosφ) and maps to depth through fixed differentiable calibration layer, architecturally removing shape-prior solution. It takes fringe order as auxiliary input, tolerating realistic decoding errors. Physics-informed (PINN) baseline comparison isolates architectural choice as key factor.
AI.result: PhiCalNet reduces object MAE 3.3x to 4.46 mm vs 14.54 mm baseline, with residual error confined to ±π wrap discontinuity (0.103% of pixels). Three-frame extension reaches 1.16 mm. Phase interpretability analysis and conformal uncertainty quantification validate approach, showing phase is most decodable internal feature and error localizes at discontinuities.
AI.conclusion: Architectural constraint forcing phase representation eliminates shape-prior shortcuts in FPP, significantly improving accuracy. The approach enables interpretable features and practical uncertainty quantification, representing substantial advancement in single-shot fringe projection profilometry.

title: FormalAnalyticGeo: A Neural-Symbolic Based Framework for Multimodal Analytic Geometry Problem Generation
summary: Math reasoning has achieved significant progress with the rapid advancement of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), however analytic geometry remains largely underexplored, primarily due to the scarcity of annotated samples. Existing diagram generation approaches struggle with analytic geometry: template methods cannot handle constraint-driven layouts, and generative models lack the geometric precision to render annotated conic curves correctly. We present FormalAnalyticGeo, a scalable framework for fully automatic generation of multimodal analytic geometry problems. Leveraging the rigor of formal languages, we design the framework around CDL (Condition Description Language), a formal intermediate representation that bridges free-form problem text with precise diagram rendering via a Signed Distance Field (SDF) engine. The framework employs four specialized LLM components in sequence: a Generator that produces diverse analytic geometry problems, a Formalizer that converts each problem into CDL for SDF-based rendering, a Measurer that extracts ground-truth answers through vision-based measurement on the rendered diagrams, and a Quality Verifier that checks outputs at three stages. Structured feedback from the Quality Verifier drives automatic retry, forming a closed loop that eliminates any need for human annotation. Applying FormalAnalyticGeo at scale yields AnalyticGeo7K, a dataset of over 7K verified multimodal problems, each with aligned text, diagram, formal annotation, and ground truth.Experiments show that the generated problems achieve a median ground-truth relative error of 0.70\%, with 82.3\% of answers falling within 5\% of the exact symbolic solution. Our framework and dataset will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: FormalAnalyticGeo is a fully automatic framework that generates multimodal analytic geometry problems using formal languages and LLMs, producing a high-quality dataset of 7K verified problems with aligned text, diagrams, and ground truth.
AI.motivation: Analytic geometry is underexplored in MLLMs due to scarcity of annotated samples. Existing diagram generation approaches struggle with analytic geometry: template methods can't handle constraint-driven layouts, and generative models lack geometric precision for rendering annotated conic curves correctly.
AI.method: Uses a formal intermediate representation called CDL (Condition Description Language) that bridges problem text with precise diagram rendering via SDF engine. Employs four specialized LLM components: Generator (produces problems), Formalizer (converts to CDL), Measurer (extracts answers via vision), and Quality Verifier (checks outputs). Forms a closed-loop system with automatic retry based on structured feedback.
AI.result: Produced AnalyticGeo7K dataset of over 7K verified multimodal problems with aligned text, diagram, formal annotation, and ground truth. Achieves median ground-truth relative error of 0.70%, with 82.3% of answers within 5% of exact symbolic solution.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables scalable generation of high-quality analytic geometry problems without human annotation, addressing a gap in multimodal reasoning. The publicly released framework and dataset will facilitate future research in analytic geometry and multimodal reasoning.

title: Qubit-Efficient Quantum Search for Hyperdimensional Decomposition via Logarithmic Encoding
summary: Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) represents symbols using high-dimensional hypervectors of dimension $D$. In hypervector decomposition, the objective is to recover $F$ constituent hypervectors, each drawn from a codebook of size $N$, from a bound target hypervector. This requires searching over $N^F$ candidate tuples, making the task computationally prohibitive at scale. Recent quantum approach provides a quadratic search advantage, but typically rely on qubit-inefficient $O(D)$-qubit hypervector representations. We propose a qubit-efficient quantum framework for HDC decomposition that reduces the representation cost to $O(\log D)$. The framework introduces logarithmic hypervector and binding encodings, together with a reversible hypervector lookup operator for circuit-level manipulation of dense hypervectors. Combined with a modified Dürr-Høyer search procedure, the method preserves $O(\sqrt{N^F})$ search complexity while substantially reducing qubit usage. Experimental results validate correct similarity computation, accurate decomposition in executable regimes, and significantly improved qubit scaling over baselines based on explicit $D$-qubit hypervector encodings, achieving up to $2{,}000\times$ fewer qubits.

AI.tldr: Quantum hyperdimensional computing framework that reduces qubit usage from O(D) to O(log D) for hypervector decomposition while maintaining quadratic search advantage.
AI.motivation: Hyperdimensional computing decomposition requires searching over N^F candidate tuples, which is computationally prohibitive. Existing quantum approaches provide quadratic search advantage but use qubit-inefficient O(D)-qubit representations, limiting scalability.
AI.method: Proposes a qubit-efficient quantum framework with: 1) logarithmic hypervector and binding encodings, 2) reversible hypervector lookup operator for circuit-level manipulation, and 3) modified Dürr-Høyer search procedure to maintain O(√(N^F)) complexity.
AI.result: Experimental validation shows correct similarity computation, accurate decomposition in executable regimes, and up to 2,000× fewer qubits compared to baseline O(D)-qubit encodings.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables practical quantum advantage for hyperdimensional computing by dramatically reducing qubit requirements while preserving search efficiency, potentially enabling scalable quantum HDC applications.

title: GRID: Grammar-Railed Decoding for Enterprise SQL Generation
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Extended version; a condensed version is under review at KDD 2027
summary: Large language models can write SQL, but enterprise deployment demands more than plausible text: outputs must be syntactically valid, must respect per-role and per-schema policy, must carry provable (not best-effort) guarantees, must not slow down as generations grow, and must leave a compliance-grade record of every decision. We present GRID (Grammar-Railed Decoding), a grammar-constrained decoding engine that keys exact next-token masks on parser configurations (lexer scan state x LALR(1) stack) rather than on token sequences, and uses the incrementally advanced LALR(1) parser itself as a viable-prefix oracle. LLM tokens are bridged to grammar terminals by a byte-level trie walk with a context-independent/context-dependent split that makes cache-key soundness hold by construction. Role-based access control is compiled into the language: role projections subset the grammar's productions and schema lexicons restrict identifier terminals, so forbidden verbs and identifiers are unreachable at mask level. Four guarantees (soundness, completeness, termination, and near-constant per-token cost) are stated with explicit preconditions and each paired with a test or benchmark. Rust kernels bring the per-token mask to a 3.6-6.7 us median, ahead of llguidance at p50 and p90 on two tokenizers with zero false rejects; per-token guard cost is position-flat at n=16,000. On Spider, constrained decoding is worth +13 execution-accuracy points at 0.5B, and one checker-guided repair pass over the provably mask-unenforceable residue (column-level policy) lifts a 7B model to 94.5% executable. A hash-chained per-token audit trail replays bit-identically with 100% tamper detection. We state plainly what the mask cannot do (distribution faithfulness, column-level RBAC, non-LALR(1) languages) and where measured cost remains.

AI.tldr: GRID (Grammar-Railed Decoding) is a grammar-constrained decoding engine that ensures SQL generation with provable guarantees, role-based access control enforcement, and tamper-proof audit trails, achieving high accuracy with near-constant per-token cost.
AI.motivation: Enterprise deployment of LLM-generated SQL requires more than plausible text - it needs syntactically valid outputs, role/schema policy compliance, provable guarantees, scalability, and compliance-grade audit trails.
AI.method: GRID uses grammar-constrained decoding that keys next-token masks on parser configurations (lexer scan state x LALR(1) stack) rather than token sequences. It bridges LLM tokens to grammar terminals via byte-level trie walks with context-independent/dependent splits. Role-based access control is compiled into the language through grammar production subsets and schema lexicon restrictions.
AI.result: Achieves 3.6-6.7 us median per-token mask generation, outperforms llguidance at p50/p90. On Spider benchmark: +13 execution-accuracy points at 0.5B, and checker-guided repair lifts 7B model to 94.5% executable. Provides hash-chained per-token audit trail with 100% tamper detection.
AI.conclusion: GRID enables enterprise-grade SQL generation with provable guarantees for syntax validity, policy compliance, and auditability while maintaining scalability. Limitations include distribution faithfulness, column-level RBAC, and non-LALR(1) languages.

title: Mirror Horizon: Viable Path Entropy as a Measure of Bounded Reflection
summary: Mirror Theory proposes that an intelligent system should be studied not only by what it represents, but by what coherent continuations it can sustain under repeated reflection. We make this claim operational through \emph{viable path entropy} (VPE), a finite-budget measure of verified continuation capacity. Given a mirror state, a rollout protocol, a verifier, and a mode map, VPE decomposes bounded capability into two parts: the probability of reaching a viable continuation and the diversity of verified continuation modes reached among successful rollouts. This paper restores the full theoretical scaffold behind the measure: intuition as local underdetermining constraint, taste as invariant-selecting pressure, reflection as taste-guided resolution of underdetermination, and geometry as the learned structure that makes future reflection stable. We then instantiate the theory in language-model reasoning experiments on GSM8K. Across Qwen2.5-Instruct models, 32 sampled rollouts per problem, and two reflection horizons, increasing the token budget from 96 to 160 substantially expands verified reachability, reduces zero-reachability, increases verified-mode entropy, and improves smoothed VPE. At 160 tokens, Qwen2.5-1.5B realizes the strongest mirror horizon among the tested models, even though Qwen2.5-3B has more parameters. This shows that mirror horizon is not parameter count, but accessible verified continuation capacity under a bounded reflection protocol. The result supports Mirror Theory as a measure-level account: capability is the structure of viable continuations made reachable, not merely one-shot accuracy or pass@k.

AI.tldr: Mirror Theory proposes measuring AI capability via viable path entropy (VPE), which quantifies verified continuation capacity under bounded reflection, showing that capability is about reachable viable continuations rather than one-shot accuracy.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to operationalize Mirror Theory's claim that intelligent systems should be studied by their ability to sustain coherent continuations under repeated reflection, moving beyond traditional measures like one-shot accuracy or pass@k.
AI.method: Develops viable path entropy (VPE) - a finite-budget measure that decomposes capability into two components: probability of reaching viable continuations and diversity of verified continuation modes. Tests on GSM8K with Qwen2.5-Instruct models using 32 sampled rollouts per problem at different reflection horizons.
AI.result: Increasing token budget from 96 to 160 tokens expands verified reachability, reduces zero-reachability, increases verified-mode entropy, and improves smoothed VPE. Qwen2.5-1.5B shows strongest mirror horizon despite having fewer parameters than Qwen2.5-3B, demonstrating that mirror horizon reflects accessible verified continuation capacity, not parameter count.
AI.conclusion: Mirror Theory provides a measure-level account where capability is defined by the structure of viable continuations made reachable under bounded reflection protocols, not merely by parameter count or traditional accuracy metrics.

title: Calibration-First Reward-Component Auditing for Reinforcement Learning Control in Smart Greenhouses
summary: Greenhouse reinforcement learning can test climate-control ideas at a speed and scale that is difficult to achieve with crop experiments alone. For smart-greenhouse control, however, a single simulator return is not enough: a grower or control engineer also needs to know when the policy heats, enriches CO2, vents, manages humidity, deploys screens, or uses lamps.We propose a reproducible calibration-first reward audit framework that keeps named greenhouse-control reward components comparable across simulator training, facility-adapted rollouts, logged Autonomous Greenhouse Challenge records, and actuator-rule distillation. In GreenLight-Gym, the framework decomposes the scalar reward into conditional temperature, CO2, humidity and vapor-pressure-deficit, screen, and actuation-proxy terms; adapts GreenLight to the second Autonomous Greenhouse Challenge logged climate traces; and scores the same components on logged greenhouse data.

AI.tldr: Proposes GreenLight-Gym, a calibration-first reward audit framework for greenhouse reinforcement learning that decomposes scalar rewards into interpretable components for comparing policies across simulations, real facilities, and competition data.
AI.motivation: Greenhouse RL can test climate-control ideas faster than crop experiments, but growers need interpretability - not just a single reward score. They need to understand when specific control actions (heating, CO2 enrichment, venting, etc.) are taken.
AI.method: A reproducible calibration-first reward audit framework that decomposes scalar reward into conditional temperature, CO2, humidity, vapor-pressure-deficit, screen, and actuation-proxy terms. Adapts GreenLight simulator to Autonomous Greenhouse Challenge data and scores components on logged greenhouse data.
AI.result: Framework enables comparable analysis of greenhouse-control reward components across simulator training, facility-adapted rollouts, logged competition records, and actuator-rule distillation. Provides interpretable policy analysis beyond just scalar rewards.
AI.conclusion: The GreenLight-Gym framework addresses the interpretability gap in greenhouse RL by providing a structured way to audit and compare control policies across different environments and real-world data, making greenhouse RL more practical for growers and engineers.

title: Mathematics of Data Science
summary:
This book is about the mathematical foundations of data science.
  1. Introduction
  2. Curses, Blessings, and Surprises in High Dimensions
  3. Singular Value Decomposition and Principal Component Analysis
  4. Linear Regression and Regularization
  5. Graphs, Networks, and Clustering
  6. Nonlinear Dimension Reduction and Diffusion Maps
  7. Linear Dimension Reduction via Random Projections
  8. Optimization for Data Science
  9. Classification
  10. A Mathematical Introduction  to Deep Learning
  11. Large Sample Limit of Graph Laplacians
  12. Community
  13. Concentration of Measure and Gaussian Analysis
  14. Matrix Concentration Inequalities
  15. Compressive Sensing and Sparsity
  16. Low-Rank Matrix Recovery

AI.tldr: A comprehensive mathematics textbook covering foundational mathematical methods for data science, from linear algebra and high-dimensional geometry to machine learning algorithms and concentration inequalities.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous mathematical foundations for modern data science, bridging the gap between practical data science applications and their underlying mathematical theory.
AI.method: Organizes mathematical concepts into thematic chapters covering: high-dimensional geometry, linear methods (SVD, PCA, regression), graph theory, dimension reduction (linear/nonlinear), optimization, classification, deep learning, graph Laplacians, concentration inequalities, and matrix recovery techniques.
AI.result: A structured mathematical curriculum covering essential topics including: curse/blessing of dimensionality, spectral methods, regularization techniques, network analysis, manifold learning, random projections, optimization algorithms, classification methods, neural network theory, statistical concentration bounds, and sparse/low-rank recovery.
AI.conclusion: Data science requires diverse mathematical foundations spanning linear algebra, probability, optimization, and geometry, with applications ranging from traditional statistical methods to modern machine learning and deep learning techniques.

title: Optimization Is Not All You Need
comment: This essay will be forthcoming in MFS Modern Fiction Studies, published by JHUP (Spring-Summer 2027)
summary: In 2019, OpenAI released two million GPT-2 outputs-ungrammatical, half broken-to aid the detection of machine-generated text. The alignment that produced their more fluent successors is usually regarded as an engineering achievement; we read it instead as the newest expression of optimization culture: the conviction, older than the technology, that measurable improvement along predefined axes exhausts the question of value. Tracing that conviction through the stack-pretraining, decoding, preference tuning, benchmarking, interface-and back through its genealogy in the audit society, we arrive at the limit: an optimization procedure can measure how improbable a piece of generated text is; it cannot tell whether that unlikelihood is error or invention. A procedure that cannot make that distinction has nonetheless, within half a decade, assumed the authority to set the protocols of legitimate language. Held for centuries by academies and schoolrooms, grammars and examiners, this authority has been given over to loss functions, reward models, benchmarks, and system prompts: an apparatus that executes the office of judgment with no capacity for judging.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that AI language model optimization represents a form of 'optimization culture' where measurable improvements along predefined axes are mistaken for genuine value, and that this apparatus now controls legitimate language protocols without real judgment capacity.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the observation that while OpenAI's GPT-2 outputs were often ungrammatical and broken, subsequent AI language models have become more fluent through optimization. The authors question this 'optimization culture' - the belief that measurable improvements along predefined metrics exhaust the question of value. They want to trace how this culture now controls what counts as legitimate language.
AI.method: The authors trace the optimization culture through the AI stack (pretraining, decoding, preference tuning, benchmarking, interface) and genealogically back through the 'audit society.' They analyze how optimization procedures measure improbability in generated text but cannot distinguish between error and invention.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that optimization procedures can measure text improbability but lack the capacity to judge whether that unlikelihood represents error or creative invention. Despite this limitation, these procedures have assumed authority over legitimate language protocols within just half a decade.
AI.conclusion: The authority over legitimate language, once held by traditional institutions (academies, schoolrooms, grammars, examiners), has been transferred to an apparatus of loss functions, reward models, benchmarks, and system prompts that executes judgment with no real capacity for judging - representing a troubling shift in linguistic authority.

title: CARE-LoRA: Compressed Activation REconstruction for Memory-Efficient LoRA
comment: 15 pages, 2 figures, 12 tables. Code available at https://github.com/fishandyu/CARE-LoRA
summary: As the scale of large pre-trained models continues to grow, fine-tuning them under limited memory budgets has become increasingly challenging. Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), currently one of the most widely adopted parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, mitigates this challenge by optimizing only low-rank adaptation matrices, thereby greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters. With the parameter overhead substantially reduced, the activations retained for backpropagation have emerged as the primary remaining memory bottleneck during LoRA fine-tuning. To address this, we propose CARE-LoRA, a data-aware Compressed Activation REconstruction framework. By exploiting the inherent projection structure of LoRA, CARE-LoRA replaces the full input activation with the low-rank compressed activation naturally produced by the LoRA branch. It further computes a lightweight reconstruction matrix during the forward pass with negligible additional computation cost, which is used during backpropagation to reconstruct the gradient signal, thereby keeping LoRA matrices fully trainable. Extensive experiments across diverse models and downstream tasks demonstrate that, while substantially reducing the overall memory footprint, CARE-LoRA achieves competitive or even superior performance compared with standard LoRA and representative LoRA variants. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/fishandyu/CARE-LoRA .

AI.tldr: CARE-LoRA: A memory-efficient LoRA variant that reduces activation memory by compressing and reconstructing activations, maintaining performance while lowering memory footprint during fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: As pre-trained models grow larger, fine-tuning under memory constraints becomes challenging. While LoRA reduces parameter overhead by optimizing low-rank adaptation matrices, activation storage for backpropagation becomes the primary memory bottleneck during LoRA fine-tuning.
AI.method: CARE-LoRA is a data-aware Compressed Activation REconstruction framework that exploits LoRA's projection structure. It replaces full input activations with low-rank compressed activations from the LoRA branch, computes lightweight reconstruction matrices during forward pass with minimal computational cost, and uses these to reconstruct gradient signals during backpropagation while keeping LoRA matrices fully trainable.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse models and downstream tasks show CARE-LoRA substantially reduces overall memory footprint while achieving competitive or superior performance compared to standard LoRA and representative LoRA variants.
AI.conclusion: CARE-LoRA effectively addresses the activation memory bottleneck in LoRA fine-tuning, offering a memory-efficient solution that maintains or improves performance, making large model fine-tuning more feasible under constrained memory budgets.

title: LP Mining with LP2Graph: A Use Case for Railway Rescheduling
comment: 22 pages, 2 figures. Work in progress, not yet submitted to a journal; comments welcome. Companion preprint to a talk at IFORS 2026, Vienna
summary: Like many optimization-driven domains, railway rescheduling relies on Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP), yet the field's modeling knowledge is scattered across hundreds of papers in incompatible notations, and narrative surveys organize it subjectively: they classify models by vocabulary rather than by structure, and reproduce neither. We present LP Mining with LP2Graph, a method that mines the structure of published LP and MILP formulations into a reproducible dataset and an induced taxonomy. Its core, LP2Graph, represents each formulation admitted by its canonical grammar as a typed variable--equation graph derived from a single canonical model; once a source is extracted into that model, everything downstream is deterministic. Each source is parsed into this model, homologized, and clustered bottom-up (over variables, then constraints and the objective, then whole-model structure) and, separately, by application domain and solution approach; the resulting groups are labeled by a rule-seeded, self-updating classifier. We validate the representation rather than assume it: per-cluster representatives are regenerated as independent LaTeX and re-solved across CBC, HiGHS and Gurobi against the optimum reported in the source paper. The outcome is an objective, repeatable taxonomy of variables, constraints and model types: the principled foundation on which our raiLPminer line of automated railway-rescheduling model development builds.

AI.tldr: LP Mining with LP2Graph automatically extracts and structures published LP/MILP formulations into a reproducible dataset with objective taxonomy, enabling systematic analysis and automated model development for railway rescheduling.
AI.motivation: Railway rescheduling relies on MILP but suffers from scattered modeling knowledge across hundreds of papers with incompatible notations. Existing surveys classify models subjectively by vocabulary rather than structure and lack reproducibility.
AI.method: LP2Graph converts LP/MILP formulations into typed variable-equation graphs using a canonical grammar. Sources are parsed, homologized, and clustered bottom-up (variables→constraints→whole models), plus by application domain and solution approach. Clusters are labeled by rule-seeded classifier and validated by regenerating LaTeX code and re-solving with multiple solvers.
AI.result: The method creates an objective, repeatable taxonomy of variables, constraints, and model types with validated representations (re-solved across CBC, HiGHS, Gurobi against original optima).
AI.conclusion: The resulting taxonomy provides a principled foundation for automated railway-rescheduling model development (raiLPminer line), addressing the field's fragmented knowledge and enabling systematic, reproducible analysis of optimization models.

title: How Query Visibility Changes KV-Cache Compression Rankings: A Matched-Budget Audit
summary: KV-cache compression methods are predominantly evaluated with the query appended to the context before compression -- a query-aware protocol. Yet the economic case for a compressed KV cache is reuse: compress a document once, answer many future questions against it. In that deployment, compression must happen query-agnostic -- before any question is seen. We present a matched-budget audit of six published compression methods against three trivial baselines on three open 7-9B models (144,300 paired evaluations on RULER-8192; 40,800 on LongBench; 50,000-resample paired bootstrap throughout). Everything is held fixed -- model, compression ratio, instances, decoding -- except the scoring rule. Three findings. (1) Query visibility changes the rankings: under the agnostic protocol, of the five audited methods that share a common attention backend, only KeyDiff beats a best-of-3 trivial baseline consistently (31 of 36 cells), and the most widely deployed method, SnapKV, loses to "keep the start and the recent window" on average (-0.066). (2) The per-method drop between the two protocols is ordered consistently with how visible the question is to each method's scoring signal, legible in its source code: from Delta=+0.198 for SnapKV (the question sits inside its 64-token observation window) down to Delta=+0.011 for KeyDiff (its score contains no query term at all).

AI.tldr: KV-cache compression methods perform differently when evaluated with query-aware (question visible) vs query-agnostic (question not visible) protocols, with most published methods underperforming simple baselines in real deployment scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current KV-cache compression methods are evaluated using query-aware protocols where queries are appended to context before compression, but real economic deployment requires query-agnostic compression where documents are compressed once and used to answer many future questions without seeing the questions first.
AI.method: Conducted matched-budget audit comparing six published compression methods against three trivial baselines on three open 7-9B models (LLaMA 7B, Mistral 7B, and their instruction-tuned variants). Evaluated 144,300 paired evaluations on RULER-8192 and 40,800 on LongBench using 50,000-resample paired bootstrap. Kept model, compression ratio, instances, and decoding fixed while varying only the scoring rule between query-aware and query-agnostic protocols.
AI.result: 1) Query visibility changes method rankings dramatically - under query-agnostic protocol, only KeyDiff consistently beats a best-of-3 trivial baseline (31 of 36 cells), while widely deployed SnapKV loses to simple "keep the start and recent window" baseline (-0.066). 2) Performance drop between protocols correlates with how visible questions are to each method's scoring signal, ranging from Delta=+0.198 for SnapKV (question visible in its 64-token window) to Delta=+0.011 for KeyDiff (no query term in scoring).
AI.conclusion: Current evaluation protocols for KV-cache compression methods don't reflect real-world deployment needs, as most published methods underperform simple baselines when evaluated query-agnostically. Future compression method design and evaluation should consider practical query-agnostic scenarios rather than query-aware setups.

title: Designing Agent-Ready Websites for AI Web Agents: A Framework for Machine Readability, Actionability, and Decision Reliability
summary: Online shopping is increasingly shifting toward a model in which AI agents independently search for products, compare options, evaluate constraints, and carry out parts of the purchasing process for users. Website design must now support both human and agent-mediated interaction. This paper introduces the agent-ready website, a design framework for enhancing the readability, interpretability, verifiability, and actionability of e-commerce platforms for AI agents. Existing web design, SEO, and generative engine optimization (GEO) metrics do not fully assess a website's capacity for agent-mediated interaction. The proposed framework is structured around three dimensions agent interpretability, agent executability, and agent decision reliability supported by features such as machine readability, semantic clarity, agent actionability, and contextual decision-reliability signals. The framework is evaluated through a controlled experiment comparing a human-oriented baseline and an agent-ready version of an identical website prototype, with identical catalogs, pricing, stock, and shopping workflows. The evaluation involved five tasks, three browser-agent models (GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5 Flash, and Grok-4 Fast), and 300 runs, measuring PASS,PARTIAL,FAIL outcomes, strict and functional success rates, error patterns, step counts, and token consumption. The agent-ready website achieved 134 PASS runs out of 150 versus 74 out of 150 for the baseline (strict success rates of 89.3% vs. 49.3%), with the largest gains in product detail extraction, comparison, and multi-constraint selection. It also reduced PARTIAL outcomes from 43 to 3 and lowered the average step count from 9.31 to 6.49. These results provide preliminary evidence that enhanced structural clarity, action cues, evidence signals, and temporal validity indicators can substantially improve the reliability and efficiency of AI browser agents.

AI.tldr: Agent-ready website framework improves AI agent performance on e-commerce sites by 40% in strict success rates and reduces task steps by 30%.
AI.motivation: With AI agents increasingly handling online shopping tasks, existing website designs optimized for humans fail to adequately support agent-mediated interaction, necessitating a new framework that enhances readability, interpretability, verifiability, and actionability for AI.
AI.method: Proposed a three-dimensional framework (agent interpretability, executability, decision reliability) with features like machine readability, semantic clarity, actionability, and decision-reliability signals. Evaluated through controlled experiment comparing baseline human-oriented website with agent-ready version using identical content and workflows.
AI.result: Agent-ready website achieved 89.3% strict success rate vs 49.3% for baseline, with 134 PASS runs vs 74 out of 150. Reduced PARTIAL outcomes from 43 to 3 and lowered average step count from 9.31 to 6.49. Largest improvements in product detail extraction, comparison, and multi-constraint selection.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced structural clarity, action cues, evidence signals, and temporal validity indicators can substantially improve reliability and efficiency of AI browser agents in e-commerce contexts, demonstrating the viability of agent-ready website design.

title: BattVAE-GP: Generative Modeling of Long-Horizon Battery Degradation with Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Long-horizon physics-based simulations of battery degradation provide mechanistic insight but remain computationally expensive, limiting their use for dense exploration of operating conditions over extended cycle life. Here, we propose a hybrid physics-probabilistic learning framework for surrogate modeling of lithium-ion battery degradation trajectories at unseen charging rates. Cycle-resolved degradation data generated with a DFN/P2D electrochemical model in PyBaMM are first transformed into capacity-aligned voltage and derivative features and encoded using a Variational Autoencoder (VAE). The resulting two-dimensional latent space organizes degradation trajectories according to both cycle progression and charging protocol. A sparse multitask Gaussian process (GP) is then trained in this latent space using cycle number and C-rate as input variables, providing continuous interpolation of latent degradation dynamics together with posterior uncertainty estimates. Under protocol-level holdout evaluation, the latent-space GP accurately recovers unseen C-rate trajectories and exhibits uncertainty behavior consistent with the support of the training data. When queried at unseen interior C-rates, the model generates latent trajectories that remain coherently positioned between neighboring simulated protocols. Decoding the GP-predicted latent states through the frozen VAE decoder yields smooth voltage-capacity evolution, while Monte Carlo propagation of the GP latent posterior through an auxiliary latent to State of Health (SOH) predictor provides uncertainty-aware SOH estimates. The proposed BattVAE-GP framework therefore offers a computationally efficient and uncertainty-aware surrogate for long-horizon degradation modeling, providing a structured basis for extending battery health prediction toward richer operating conditions and future simulation-experiment fusion.

AI.tldr: BattVAE-GP: A hybrid physics-probabilistic learning framework that combines Variational Autoencoders with Gaussian Processes to create efficient, uncertainty-aware surrogate models for predicting lithium-ion battery degradation at unseen charging rates.
AI.motivation: Physics-based simulations of battery degradation are computationally expensive, limiting dense exploration of operating conditions over extended cycle life. There's a need for efficient surrogate models that can predict degradation trajectories at unseen charging conditions with uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Transform cycle-resolved degradation data (from DFN/P2D electrochemical model) into capacity-aligned voltage/derivative features, encode with Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to 2D latent space, then train a sparse multitask Gaussian Process (GP) in latent space using cycle number and C-rate as inputs to interpolate degradation dynamics with uncertainty.
AI.result: The framework accurately recovers unseen C-rate trajectories with uncertainty consistent with training data support, generates coherent latent trajectories for unseen interior C-rates, and provides smooth voltage-capacity evolution with uncertainty-aware State of Health estimates via Monte Carlo propagation.
AI.conclusion: BattVAE-GP offers a computationally efficient, uncertainty-aware surrogate for long-horizon degradation modeling, providing a structured basis for extending battery health prediction to richer operating conditions and enabling future simulation-experiment fusion.

title: Generalized Distribution-Free Semi-Supervised Learning with Risk Rewrite
comment: Accepted to The Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI) 2026
summary: Typical semi-supervised learning (SSL) methods rely on distributional assumptions, and their performance degrades when these are violated. While PNU learning, a risk rewriting method, offers a distribution-free alternative, it is restricted to binary classification and its variance optimality remains unclear. In this paper, we propose a generalized framework that constructs unbiased risk estimators using linear combinations of component risks, subsuming PNU learning and extending to multiclass classification. We derive the minimum achievable variance, demonstrating our estimator can attain lower variance than PNU in asymmetric loss scenarios. Furthermore, we establish a generalization bound directly linking this variance reduction to improved learning performance. Based on these theoretical insights, we introduce two practical SSL methods that empirically match or outperform existing approaches on binary and multiclass benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a generalized framework for semi-supervised learning that constructs unbiased risk estimators using linear combinations of component risks, extending distribution-free PNU learning to multiclass classification while reducing variance and improving performance.
AI.motivation: Typical SSL methods rely on distributional assumptions and fail when these assumptions are violated. While PNU learning offers a distribution-free alternative, it's limited to binary classification and its variance optimality is unclear. There's a need for a more general, theoretically sound framework that works for multiclass classification with better variance properties.
AI.method: The authors propose a generalized framework that constructs unbiased risk estimators using linear combinations of component risks. They derive the minimum achievable variance for these estimators and establish generalization bounds linking variance reduction to improved learning performance. They also introduce two practical SSL methods based on these theoretical insights.
AI.result: The proposed estimator can attain lower variance than PNU learning in asymmetric loss scenarios. Theoretical analysis shows the framework subsumes PNU learning and extends to multiclass classification. The practical SSL methods empirically match or outperform existing approaches on both binary and multiclass benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a theoretically grounded generalization of distribution-free SSL that overcomes the limitations of PNU learning, enabling multiclass classification with reduced variance and improved empirical performance across various benchmark tasks.

title: Operationalising Multi-Dimensional Evaluation for Conversational Agents: A Scalable, Governed Pipeline with Selective Re-evaluation and Model Benchmarking
comment: 14 pages, 1 figure of design of architecture and 2 tables exploring the results and benchmarking
summary: Evaluating retail conversational agents requires methods beyond lexical-overlap metrics to assess intent alignment, factuality, helpfulness, clarity, tone, and overall response quality. Although LLM-as-a-judge methods provide scalable alternatives to human evaluation, production deployment introduces challenges in governance, reproducibility, cost, schema consistency, traceability, and reliability. We present GenAI Evaluation, a governed, configuration-driven pipeline for large-scale evaluation of retail conversational systems. It processes production chatbot logs through normalization, sharding, asynchronous execution, and schema-constrained LLM scoring. The framework evaluates helpfulness, truthfulness, clarity, tone alignment, and translation-specific dimensions. Selective re-evaluation processes only incomplete, malformed, or schema-invalid records, while schema locking, versioned configurations, validation logs, and record-level provenance support auditability. The framework processes approximately 50,000 records daily and has evaluated more than two million interactions. Validation used 12,980 stratified-random human-labeled records from four trained annotators. Classification covered 14 intents, 156 sub-intents, 18 major domains, and 129 sub-domains. The pipeline achieved a macro F1 score of 0.93 and 89% human-acceptability accuracy for translation.

AI.tldr: GenAI Evaluation is a governed, configuration-driven pipeline for large-scale evaluation of retail conversational agents, addressing challenges in LLM-as-a-judge deployment while processing ~50k daily records with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional lexical-overlap metrics are insufficient for evaluating conversational agents, and while LLM-as-a-judge methods offer scalability, they face production challenges including governance, reproducibility, cost, schema consistency, traceability, and reliability issues.
AI.method: A governed, configuration-driven pipeline that processes production chatbot logs through normalization, sharding, asynchronous execution, and schema-constrained LLM scoring. It evaluates helpfulness, truthfulness, clarity, tone alignment, and translation-specific dimensions with selective re-evaluation for incomplete/malformed records.
AI.result: The framework processes approximately 50,000 records daily and has evaluated over two million interactions. Validation using 12,980 human-labeled records achieved macro F1 score of 0.93 and 89% human-acceptability accuracy for translation, covering 14 intents, 156 sub-intents, 18 major domains, and 129 sub-domains.
AI.conclusion: GenAI Evaluation successfully addresses production deployment challenges for LLM-based evaluation of retail conversational agents, providing a scalable, governed, and auditable solution with high accuracy comparable to human evaluation.

title: Scale-Aware Attention for Scarce Neural Data: An RG-Flow Transformer on Sleep-EDF EEG
summary: Brain field potentials are scale-free: their power spectra follow a $1/f^β$ law whose aperiodic exponent $β$ tracks cortical state, and sleep depth in particular is a shift in $β$. We ask whether a transformer endowed with an explicit renormalization-group (RG) inductive bias -- the RG-Flow Transformer, which couples ordinary self-attention to a scale-aware stream with a learnable anomalous dimension $γ$, block-spin coarse-graining, and an entropy-gated synchronization bridge -- has an advantage over a parameter-matched vanilla transformer on \emph{real, scarce} EEG. Using the PhysioNet Sleep-EDF corpus with a strict leakage-free by-subject hold-out, we (i) benchmark RG-Flow against a param-matched vanilla transformer and a hierarchy-only ablation on 5-class AASM sleep staging, (ii) sweep the per-subject data budget to look for the inductive-bias crossover predicted when data are scarce, and (iii) test whether RG-Flow's learned $γ$ tracks the measured spectral exponent $β$ out-of-sample -- a quantity the vanilla model does not possess. Across $5$ subjects and $5$ seeds under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation, RG-Flow and the vanilla transformer are statistically indistinguishable on 5-class staging (77.3\% vs 77.0\% accuracy; paired $p=0.294$), and the predicted scarce-data crossover does not appear: vanilla is numerically ahead at every data-limited budget. What does separate the models is interpretability -- RG-Flow recovers the continuous spectral exponent out-of-sample ($β$-recovery $R^2 = 0.416$), a capability the vanilla architecture has no analogue for.

AI.tldr: RG-Flow Transformer with renormalization-group inductive bias doesn't outperform vanilla transformer on EEG sleep staging, but learns interpretable spectral exponents.
AI.motivation: EEG brain signals have scale-free properties with 1/f^β power spectra, and sleep depth correlates with β shifts. The authors want to test if a transformer with explicit renormalization-group (RG) inductive bias can better model these scale-invariant EEG patterns compared to vanilla transformers, especially with scarce real EEG data.
