AI.result: JoPMol outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple evaluation metrics, demonstrates strong generalization ability in both transfer tasks and biologically grounded simulation scenarios, and shows effectiveness for precision molecular design.
AI.conclusion: JoPMol provides an effective unified framework for coordinated generation and optimization of candidate molecules under joint condition control, advancing precision molecular design for personalized drug discovery. The model successfully integrates biological, structural, and chemical information sources.

title: EVOQUANT: Self-Evolving Verifier-Guided Strategy Optimization for Robust Quantitative Trading
summary: Quantitative strategy optimization remains largely manual, requiring domain experts to identify weak signals, tune risk-control rules, and repeatedly validate iterative revisions. Large language models can accelerate this process, but directly relying on them to rewrite trading strategies often introduces hallucinated edits, strategy drift, and backtest overfitting. We propose EVOQUANT, a self-Evolving Verifier-guided framework for strategy Optimization in Quantitative trading. Our method utilizes LLMs to deeply diagnose performance bottlenecks, generates semantically controlled candidate edits, selects the best strategy through a multi-stage verification pipeline, and distills optimization experience into reusable knowledge for continual self-improvement. We evaluate our method using seven representative strategies: four from the A-share market and three from the Crypto market. Experimental results show that our method significantly improves the Sharpe ratio across all tested strategies: the average test Sharpe increases from -0.298 to 0.538, and the best-performing strategy achieves a 199% relative improvement. Ablation studies and stress tests under stricter conditions further validate the effectiveness and robustness of the framework. Overall, this work transforms quantitative strategy optimization from costly manual trial and error into an automated and verifiable iterative paradigm, offering a new path for applying large language models to financial strategy research.

AI.tldr: EVOQUANT is a self-evolving verifier-guided framework that uses LLMs to automate quantitative strategy optimization, improving Sharpe ratios across multiple market strategies while avoiding common pitfalls like hallucination and overfitting.
AI.motivation: Quantitative strategy optimization is largely manual, requiring domain experts to identify weak signals, tune rules, and validate revisions, while directly using LLMs to rewrite strategies often causes hallucinated edits, strategy drift, and backtest overfitting.
AI.method: EVOQUANT uses LLMs to diagnose performance bottlenecks, generate semantically controlled candidate edits, select the best strategy via a multi-stage verification pipeline, and distill optimization experience into reusable knowledge for continual self-improvement.
AI.result: Evaluation on seven strategies (four A-share, three Crypto) shows significant Sharpe ratio improvements: average test Sharpe increases from -0.298 to 0.538, with the best strategy achieving a 199% relative improvement. Ablation studies and stress tests confirm effectiveness and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The EVOQUANT framework successfully automates quantitative strategy optimization, reducing manual effort and improving performance metrics like Sharpe ratio, demonstrating a robust and verifiable iterative paradigm for applying LLMs in financial strategy research.

title: Sparse Inter-Layer Dependencies of Transformer FFN Neurons
summary: Feedforward network (FFN) blocks account for a large fraction of the parameters and computation in Transformer architectures, yet their internal structure remains difficult to interpret due to the additive superposition induced by the residual stream. We examine whether the activation of an FFN neuron can be explained by a sparse set of preceding neuron activations and attention outputs. We introduce a training-free attribution method that estimates the relative influence of upstream neurons and attention outputs on a target neuron's activation. Empirically, across models and layers, we find that small subsets of preceding activations and attention outputs suffice to preserve neuron activations with high fidelity when all remaining inputs are masked with their average values. Effective sparsity is even greater when accounting for the inherent activation sparsity of upstream layers. Moreover, applying the neuron-specific masks in all layers simultaneously, such that the induced deviations propagate through the network, leaves model perplexity largely unchanged at moderate sparsity levels. These results demonstrate that, despite dense parameterization, FFNs exhibit sparse and structured inter-layer dependencies at the neuron level. Our method provides a practical, scalable tool for circuit-level interpretability and identifies candidate sparse pathways with potential implications for efficient inference.

AI.tldr: FFN blocks in Transformers show sparse neuron-level dependencies despite dense parameterization, with a training-free attribution method revealing that small subsets of upstream activations can explain target neuron behavior with high fidelity.
AI.motivation: FFN blocks contain most parameters/computation in Transformers but are hard to interpret due to residual stream superposition. The authors want to understand if FFN neuron activations can be explained by sparse subsets of preceding neurons and attention outputs.
AI.method: The authors introduce a training-free attribution method that estimates relative influence of upstream neurons and attention outputs on target neuron activation. They empirically test this across models and layers by masking non-influential inputs with average values.
AI.result: Small subsets of preceding activations suffice to preserve neuron activations with high fidelity. Effective sparsity is even greater considering upstream activation sparsity. When applying neuron-specific masks across all layers simultaneously, model perplexity remains largely unchanged at moderate sparsity levels.
AI.conclusion: Despite dense parameterization, FFNs exhibit sparse and structured inter-layer dependencies at neuron level. The method provides a practical tool for circuit-level interpretability and identifies sparse pathways potentially useful for efficient inference.

title: Do We Really Need Transformers for Global Spatial Information Extraction in Traffic Forecasting?
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Source code available at https://github.com/uuesti/U-Trans
summary: Existing traffic forecasting models commonly focus on extracting spatial dependencies, particularly global spatial information, which characterizes the representations obtained through interactions between each individual node and all nodes across the traffic network. However, the underlying mechanism by which such global information is modeled and extracted remains insufficiently investigated. Whether global information must be extracted by high-degree-of-freedom adaptive attention or can be captured by a simple global aggregation operator remains unclear. For this purpose, we design a controlled ablation framework that replaces only the spatial mixing module to test attention-based global interaction. Across six traffic benchmarks, uniform full-range mixing and standard spatial attention each achieve lower MAE on three datasets, with only a 0.14% difference in mean MAE, while the former reduces node-scale spatial mixing complexity from O(N2) to O(N). Mechanism analysis further decomposes spatial attention into a row-uniform global background and a non-uniform residual. The residual shows dataset-dependent marginal value, suggesting that spatial attention should be justified by stable gains beyond a row-uniform global background. The corresponding source code is publicly available at: https://github.com/uuesti/U-Trans

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether global spatial information in traffic forecasting can be captured by simple global aggregation operators instead of complex adaptive attention mechanisms, finding that uniform full-range mixing performs comparably to spatial attention with lower complexity.
AI.motivation: Existing traffic forecasting models focus on extracting spatial dependencies and global spatial information, but the underlying mechanism for modeling this global information is insufficiently investigated. It's unclear whether global information requires complex adaptive attention or can be captured by simpler global aggregation operators.
AI.method: The authors design a controlled ablation framework that replaces only the spatial mixing module to test attention-based global interactions. They compare uniform full-range mixing (simple global aggregation) with standard spatial attention across six traffic benchmarks, analyzing complexity and decomposing spatial attention components.
AI.result: Across six traffic benchmarks, uniform full-range mixing and standard spatial attention each achieve lower MAE on three datasets, with only a 0.14% difference in mean MAE. The uniform mixing reduces node-scale spatial mixing complexity from O(N²) to O(N). Mechanism analysis shows spatial attention decomposes into a row-uniform global background and a non-uniform residual, where the residual shows dataset-dependent marginal value.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that spatial attention mechanisms should be justified by demonstrating stable gains beyond what can be achieved by a simple row-uniform global background. Simple global aggregation operators can perform comparably to complex attention mechanisms while offering significant computational efficiency benefits.

title: Mitigating The Effect of Class Imbalance in Data with Hierarchical and Dependable Structure
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables; preprint submitted to IEEE COMPSAC 2026
summary: Classifying cybersecurity vulnerabilities using the Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) taxonomy is challenging due to extreme class imbalance and strong hierarchical dependencies among weakness categories. Although oversampling techniques such as Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and Adaptive Synthetic Sampling (ADASYN) are widely adopted to mitigate class imbalance, their effectiveness for hierarchical CWE text classification remains largely unexplored. This paper proposes a Hierarchy-Aware RoBERTa framework that explicitly incorporates CWE structural information through learnable parent-class embeddings, preserving taxonomic consistency. Our experiments demonstrate that synthetic interpolation in high-dimensional embedding spaces violates the inherent parent-child constraints of the CWE hierarchy, offering only marginal benefits for classical ML models while consistently degrading deep learning architectures. Evaluated on a CWE Research Concept dataset, the proposed model achieves a weighted F1-score of 0.76 without data augmentation, outperforming all baselines with notable gains on minority classes, including the Class category whose F1-score improved from 0.40 to 0.60 over the BERT baseline. Our results suggest that hierarchy-aware representation learning is a more principled alternative to oversampling for structured vulnerability classification.

AI.tldr: Hierarchy-aware RoBERTa framework outperforms oversampling methods for CWE vulnerability classification by incorporating structural information and addressing hierarchical constraints.
AI.motivation: Classifying cybersecurity vulnerabilities using CWE taxonomy is challenging due to extreme class imbalance and strong hierarchical dependencies among weakness categories. The effectiveness of oversampling techniques like SMOTE and ADASYN for hierarchical CWE text classification remains largely unexplored.
AI.method: Proposes a Hierarchy-Aware RoBERTa framework that explicitly incorporates CWE structural information through learnable parent-class embeddings, preserving taxonomic consistency. This avoids synthetic interpolation that violates parent-child constraints in high-dimensional embedding spaces.
AI.result: The proposed model achieves a weighted F1-score of 0.76 without data augmentation, outperforming all baselines with notable gains on minority classes. Class category F1-score improved from 0.40 to 0.60 over BERT baseline. Synthetic interpolation consistently degrades deep learning architectures while offering only marginal benefits for classical ML models.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchy-aware representation learning is a more principled alternative to oversampling for structured vulnerability classification, as it respects the inherent parent-child constraints of CWE hierarchy and outperforms traditional data augmentation methods.

title: Function-Aware Fill-in-the-Middle as Mid-Training for Coding Agent Foundation Models
summary: Coding agents must integrate external tool returns into ongoing reasoning - a capability that standard left-to-right pretraining on code exposes only in its forward direction. We observe that the action-observation-continuation loop of a coding agent is structurally isomorphic to a function call site, where a caller binds arguments, a callee returns a value computed elsewhere, and downstream code consumes that value. This conditioning structure exists at internet scale in ordinary code. We exploit it through function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training: a self-supervised objective that masks functions selected via program dependency graph analysis and a complexity-inferability double criterion. We mid-train Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct (7B/14B) and Qwen3-8B on a 2.6B-token decontaminated corpus drawn from 968 GitHub repositories, then apply existing agentic post-training pipelines. Mid-training improves SWE-Bench-Verified by +2.8/+3.0 at 7B/14B and by +3.2 on Qwen3-8B; SWE-Bench-Lite gains are +3.7/+4.0/+5.4 on the same models. The improvement holds across two post-training pipelines (R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith) and on a non-Qwen2.5 base (Qwen3-8B with SWE-Lego). Beyond in-domain gains, mid-training also mitigates the capability erosion that agentic post-training otherwise inflicts on non-agent coding (e.g., LiveCodeBench) and non-coding tool-use benchmarks (tau-bench, BFCL): although the mid-training corpus contains Python code only, the function-call inductive bias survives post-training and yields consistent gains.

AI.tldr: Function-aware fill-in-the-middle mid-training improves coding agents by leveraging the structural similarity between agent action-observation loops and function calls in ordinary code, boosting performance on SWE-Bench and mitigating capability erosion.
AI.motivation: Standard left-to-right pretraining on code only exposes forward reasoning, but coding agents need to integrate external tool returns into ongoing reasoning. The action-observation-continuation loop of coding agents is structurally isomorphic to function call sites in ordinary code, creating an opportunity for better training.
AI.method: Function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training: self-supervised objective that masks functions selected via program dependency graph analysis and a complexity-inferability double criterion. Applied to Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct (7B/14B) and Qwen3-8B models using a 2.6B-token decontaminated corpus from 968 GitHub repositories, followed by existing agentic post-training pipelines.
AI.result: Significant improvements on SWE-Bench: +2.8/+3.0 at 7B/14B and +3.2 on Qwen3-8B for SWE-Bench-Verified; +3.7/+4.0/+5.4 for SWE-Bench-Lite. Gains hold across different post-training pipelines (R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith). Mid-training also mitigates capability erosion on non-agent coding (LiveCodeBench) and non-coding tool-use benchmarks (tau-bench, BFCL), even though training corpus contains only Python code.
AI.conclusion: Function-aware FIM mid-training effectively improves coding agent performance by leveraging the structural similarity between agent reasoning patterns and function calls in code, providing robust improvements across multiple benchmarks while preventing capability degradation from agentic post-training.

title: Are we Merging the Right Models? Impact of Expert Training Duration on Model Merging for LLMs
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 workshop on weight-space symmetries
summary: Multi-task model merging combines separately trained expert models into a single model that handles all tasks without co-training. Standard practice merges experts at their optimal validation loss. We challenge this convention by systematically studying how training duration of domain experts affects the quality of the merged model. We fine-tune experts on five domains (Math, Code, Instruction Following, Multilingual, and Safety) across three model sizes (Qwen 3.5 0.8B, 2B, and 4B), saving checkpoints from 25\% to 500\% of the optimal training steps and evaluating five merging methods at each duration. Our findings reveal a striking method-dependent pattern: simple averaging degrades sharply with overfitting, while sparsification-based methods achieve their best performance well past the validation optimum. We formalize this through bias-variance decomposition analysis, drawing a parallel to random forests where averaging benefits from high-variance individual learners. These results suggest that training duration and merging method should be chosen jointly rather than independently.

AI.tldr: Multi-task model merging performance depends on both training duration and merging method - simple averaging degrades with overfitting while sparsification-based methods peak after validation optimum.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the standard practice of merging expert models at their optimal validation loss, questioning whether training duration of domain experts affects merged model quality.
AI.method: Systematic study fine-tuning experts on five domains (Math, Code, Instruction Following, Multilingual, Safety) across three model sizes, saving checkpoints from 25% to 500% of optimal training steps and evaluating five merging methods at each duration.
AI.result: Reveals method-dependent pattern: simple averaging degrades sharply with overfitting, while sparsification-based methods achieve best performance well past validation optimum. Bias-variance decomposition shows parallels to random forests where averaging benefits from high-variance learners.
AI.conclusion: Training duration and merging method should be chosen jointly rather than independently, as the optimal merging point differs significantly between different merging techniques.

title: From Observation to Insight: Mechanistic World Models and the Quest for Autonomous Discovery
summary: Recent advances in foundation models have transformed AI for Science, enabling remarkably accurate predictive performance across domains ranging from protein folding to weather forecasting. Yet prediction alone does not constitute scientific discovery. Scientific understanding depends on uncovering the reusable explanatory mechanisms that generate observations, whereas contemporary machine learning remains fundamentally organised around predictive mappings rather than explanatory structure. In this paper, we argue that scientific discovery is fundamentally a problem of knowledge organisation. To this end, we introduce Mechanistic World Models, a new design paradigm that places reusable mechanisms at the centre of representation, computation and learning. Drawing on insights from the philosophy of science, we derive the computational capabilities required for discovery, identify the design principles and inductive pressures that encourage explanatory knowledge to emerge, and formalise the anatomy of a mechanism-centric world model. Finally, we show how diverse research directions including mechanistic interpretability, causal representation learning, equation discovery and modular architectures capture complementary ingredients of this paradigm while lacking a unified framework. We propose Mechanistic World Models as a conceptual foundation and computational blueprint for moving AI beyond predictive forecasting towards autonomous scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: Mechanistic World Models propose a new AI paradigm focused on discovering reusable explanatory mechanisms for scientific discovery, rather than just predictive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current AI foundation models excel at prediction but lack explanatory power for scientific discovery. Scientific understanding requires uncovering reusable explanatory mechanisms, which contemporary ML organized around predictive mappings cannot provide.
AI.method: Introduces Mechanistic World Models as a design paradigm placing reusable mechanisms at the center of representation, computation, and learning. Derives required computational capabilities from philosophy of science, identifies design principles and inductive pressures for explanatory knowledge emergence, and formalizes mechanism-centric world model anatomy.
AI.result: Shows that current research directions (mechanistic interpretability, causal representation learning, equation discovery, modular architectures) capture complementary ingredients of this paradigm but lack unified framework.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic World Models provide conceptual foundation and computational blueprint for moving AI beyond predictive forecasting toward autonomous scientific discovery.

title: Institutional Equity Holdings Prediction Using Node Affinities of Dynamic Graphs
summary: Institutional equity holdings disclosed in SEC Form 13F filings provide a rich temporal record of portfolio decisions by large investment managers. However, forecasting future allocations and modeling future demand remains challenging due to disclosure lags, reporting noise, and strong persistence in institutional behavior. We introduce the first benchmark for these tasks using temporal graph machine learning, framing holdings prediction as node affinity prediction -- i.e., forecasting portfolio weights -- on a discrete-time temporal bipartite graph of managers and securities extracted from preprocessed filings. On a sampled dataset comprising 99 managers and the S\&P 500 index (503 securities, 209,351 temporal edges across 48 quarters from 2013--2025), Node Affinity prediction model using Virtual State (NAVIS) achieves a state-of-the-art test Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) of 0.9127 with features (0.9121 without), outperforming all dynamic graph representation learning competitors by a substantial margin, and outperforming all heuristic methods. Remarkably, a simple Exponential Moving Average baseline achieves 0.8882, surpassing all dynamic graph models except NAVIS and all heuristics except Persistent Forecast (0.8891), highlighting the strong smoothness and persistence of institutional portfolios. Domain-specific node features provide only marginal gains (<1.2\%), indicating that temporal and structural signals in the 13F ownership graph already capture most of the predictable information. By benchmarking a suite of Temporal Graph Benchmark (TGB) models under the node affinity prediction setting, both with and without features, on real-world 13F data, this work provides a reproducible foundation for temporal graph machine learning in holdings prediction and portfolio allocation.

AI.tldr: NAVIS introduces the first temporal graph ML benchmark for predicting institutional equity holdings from SEC 13F filings, framing it as node affinity prediction and achieving state-of-the-art results that outperform dynamic graph models and heuristics.
AI.motivation: Institutional equity holdings from SEC Form 13F filings provide rich temporal data, but forecasting future allocations is challenging due to disclosure lags, reporting noise, and strong behavioral persistence. There's a need for systematic benchmarks to model institutional demand and portfolio decisions.
AI.method: Frames holdings prediction as node affinity prediction (forecasting portfolio weights) on a discrete-time temporal bipartite graph of managers and securities extracted from 13F filings. Introduces NAVIS (Node Affinity prediction model using Virtual State) and benchmarks TGB models with/without features on sampled dataset of 99 managers and S&P 500 securities across 48 quarters.
AI.result: NAVIS achieves state-of-the-art test NDCG of 0.9127 (0.9121 without features), outperforming all dynamic graph representation learning competitors and heuristics. Simple Exponential Moving Average baseline scores 0.8882, surpassing all dynamic graph models except NAVIS, highlighting institutional portfolio smoothness. Domain features provide marginal gains (<1.2%).
AI.conclusion: Temporal and structural signals in the 13F ownership graph capture most predictable information. The work establishes a reproducible foundation for temporal graph machine learning in holdings prediction, with practical implications for forecasting institutional demand and portfolio allocation.

title: TRACE: An Operational Reasoning Schema for Auditable Agentic Commitments
summary: This paper defines TRACE (Typed Reasoning And Commitment Evidence): a typed, versioned schema for recording reasoning traces, a reference procedure for writing records against it, and one operating discipline, no durable state change without a record. The paper argues in three layers that reasoning is not in the language model: the autoregressive mechanism natively computes association; chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning inherit its limits; and the formal constructs of reasoning theory, from Socratic procedure to Pearl's ladder, are absent as machinery. The schema answers the absence with fields and tests: the TraceRecord and its causal specialization, an eight-stage reference writer, a gate-first measurement regime, the TRACE-Bench protocol, and the consumers, memory admission, plan gating, temporal regret, and verdict reuse, whose more auditable decisions are the measure of the record. A record-consumer contract states what a record guarantees and what a consumer must honor in return, making the schema an operational interface rather than a passive document. Two worked examples run in the main text: a music-lessons argument traced from sentence to typed verdict, separating association, intervention, and prescription; and a flood search-and-rescue vignette in which a predictive world model reports confident plan success that its own support and out-of-distribution scores contradict, so the record defers the commitment, requests a bounded observation, revises append-only, and clears a different branch. The vignette is illustrative, not empirical; closed-loop evaluation is left to future work, so the contribution is the schema and its contract, not a performance claim. Appendices carry the full schema, writer algorithms and cost model, clinical and policy illustrations, the benchmark protocol, convergence metrics, and usage scenarios.

AI.tldr: TRACE proposes a typed schema for recording reasoning traces with a "no durable state change without a record" principle, arguing that reasoning is not inherent to language models and providing operational interfaces for auditability.
AI.motivation: The paper argues that current language models don't actually perform reasoning - they compute associations through autoregressive mechanisms, and methods like chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning inherit these limits. Formal reasoning constructs from theory are absent in current LM architectures, creating a need for explicit reasoning trace recording.
AI.method: Defines TRACE schema with typed, versioned records; an 8-stage reference writer; gate-first measurement; TRACE-Bench protocol; and record-consumer contract. Includes worked examples of music-lessons argument tracing and flood search-and-rescue vignette to demonstrate separation of association, intervention, and prescription.
AI.result: Presents a complete schema system for recording reasoning traces, operational contracts for consumers, and practical examples. No empirical results - the contribution is conceptual/theoretical framework with schema definitions and contracts rather than performance claims.
AI.conclusion: TRACE provides a foundational schema and operational interface for making reasoning auditable in AI systems, addressing the absence of formal reasoning constructs in current models through explicit trace recording and consumer contracts.

title: Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretable Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Reliable detection of out-of-distribution (OOD) samples is crucial for the safe deployment of machine learning models. Neural networks often produce overconfident predictions for inputs that deviate from their training data, leading to significant degradation in performance. While many OOD detection methods focus on the final output layer, they neglect the rich hierarchical information present in intermediate network layers. This paper introduces a novel approach that leverages sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to learn interpretable features from these intermediate activations. We find that in-distribution (ID) and OOD data activate distinct sets of these sparse features. We propose a new OOD score derived from the cosine similarity between the sparse feature activations of a test sample and the mean activations of ID classes. Our post-hoc detection method not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard OOD detection benchmarks, but yields interpretable insights into how distribution shift affects learned representations.

AI.tldr: Using sparse autoencoders on intermediate neural network layers enables effective OOD detection by identifying distinct sparse feature activation patterns between ID and OOD data.
AI.motivation: Current OOD detection methods often fail because neural networks produce overconfident predictions for OOD samples, leading to performance degradation. Existing approaches primarily focus on final output layers, ignoring valuable hierarchical information in intermediate layers that could better capture distributional differences.
AI.method: The paper introduces a novel post-hoc OOD detection method that uses sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to extract interpretable features from intermediate network activations. It discovers that ID and OOD data activate different sets of these sparse features. A new OOD scoring mechanism is proposed based on cosine similarity between test sample sparse feature activations and mean ID class activations.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard OOD detection benchmarks. Additionally, it provides interpretable insights into how distribution shift affects learned representations, going beyond mere detection to offer understanding of the underlying mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging sparse autoencoders on intermediate layers provides both effective OOD detection and interpretability, addressing limitations of existing methods by utilizing richer hierarchical information from neural networks beyond final outputs.

title: The Model Knows Your Project, Not You: Measuring Recognition in LLMs with NameRank
summary: What a frontier model recalls about a person or tool from its own weights -- before any retrieval step -- often shapes the first description a human sees, making that parametric corpus presence a measurement problem. Citations explain about a third of whether a model recognizes a researcher; we target the residual and build NameRank, a [0,1] recognition score: each of 4,685 entities in 54 cohorts is probed with one open-ended question across 36 models, and an independent judge returns a binary verdict against a curated gold -- did the model state a specific, non-guessable fact about this exact entity? -- so hallucination, context echo, and guesses earn nothing. Synthetic-null entities hold the floor near zero, and verdicts track the entity, not the model. One thesis organizes the findings: recognition is paid to named, indexable artifacts, not to credentials or titles. Every Olympic-style credential sits below a working-researcher baseline, because no named artifact ships with the medal, yet the ranking inverts at the marquee tier, where Nobel, Turing, and Fields laureates saturate the panel. For independent creators the tool out-ranks its maker, and the credential that does propagate is a named method or awarded paper. Being one of many named contributors to a celebrated artifact, by contrast, earns almost nothing -- the authors listed on a flagship model report or system card sit near the recognition floor -- because recognition attaches to the artifact's own distinctive name, not to the roster behind it. No bibliometric predicts recognition well; top-density institutions out-recognize peers at matched citations; and on 258 news events recognition loads on peak salience, not persistence. A self-report probe shows introspection reads a corpus prior, not its own knowledge.

AI.tldr: NameRank measures how well AI models can recognize specific entities based solely on their parametric knowledge, revealing that recognition attaches to named artifacts rather than credentials or titles.
AI.motivation: To understand what AI models can recall about specific people or tools from their training weights before any retrieval step, since this initial description shapes human perception and represents a measurement problem of parametric corpus presence.
AI.method: Developed NameRank, a [0,1] recognition score by probing 4,685 entities across 54 cohorts with open-ended questions across 36 models, using independent binary judgments against curated gold standards to filter out hallucinations, context echoes, and guesses.
AI.result: Recognition attaches to named, indexable artifacts, not credentials or titles; synthetic-null entities score near zero; credentials like awards rank below working researchers unless they come with named artifacts; tools out-rank creators; bibliometrics poorly predict recognition; peak salience matters more than persistence.
AI.conclusion: AI recognition in frontier models primarily recognizes named artifacts rather than credentials, titles, or rosters of contributors, with introspection revealing corpus priors rather than actual knowledge, challenging assumptions about how models encode and recall information.

title: Evidence-Grounded AI for Musculoskeletal Care
summary: Musculoskeletal diseases are among the leading causes of disability worldwide and create the greatest global need for rehabilitation. Because recovery, remodelling and degeneration often unfold over months to years, musculoskeletal care requires longitudinal management that repeatedly integrates evolving patient evidence, external medical knowledge and stage-specific functional goals. In routine practice, this evidence is fragmented across visits, departments and hospital systems, limiting individualized, evidence-based care. Here we report OrthoPilot, a clinical artificial intelligence system powered by a large language model that integrates hospital data streams with authoritative external knowledge for continuous musculoskeletal management. OrthoPilot autonomously retrieves real-time imaging, laboratory, pathology and order data and converts evolving patient states into evidence-based decisions from admission diagnosis to rehabilitation planning. We established a specialist-validated benchmark from real-world electronic health records spanning 1,000 disease codes. In a reader study across the complete care pathway, OrthoPilot was compared with 81 orthopaedic physicians and surpassed experts with 25 years of experience in diagnostic reasoning, clinical decision-making and management planning. It also outperformed all evaluated intelligent systems across 60 external clinical centres. In a prospective study of 1,870 complex cases, OrthoPilot increased full-chain management success by 10.6%. During an 8-month randomised deployment involving 8,240 inpatients, it increased cumulative cases per bed by 9.7% and improved patient-reported access to health information. These results move clinical AI from predicting isolated events toward executing longitudinal management across complete musculoskeletal care pathways.

AI.tldr: OrthoPilot is an AI system using large language models to continuously manage musculoskeletal diseases by integrating real-time hospital data with medical knowledge, outperforming expert physicians and improving care efficiency.
AI.motivation: Musculoskeletal diseases are leading causes of disability requiring long-term management. Current clinical evidence is fragmented across different visits, departments, and systems, limiting personalized, evidence-based care. There's a need for AI that can integrate continuous patient data with medical knowledge throughout the complete care pathway.
AI.method: Developed OrthoPilot, a clinical AI system powered by large language models that integrates hospital data streams (imaging, labs, pathology, orders) with external medical knowledge. Established a specialist-validated benchmark from real-world EHRs spanning 1,000 disease codes. Evaluated through reader studies with 81 orthopaedic physicians, comparisons across 60 clinical centers, prospective study of 1,870 complex cases, and randomized deployment involving 8,240 inpatients.
AI.result: Outperformed expert physicians with 25 years experience in diagnostic reasoning, clinical decision-making, and management planning. Beat all evaluated intelligent systems across 60 external clinical centers. Increased full-chain management success by 10.6% in complex cases. During randomized deployment, increased cumulative cases per bed by 9.7% and improved patient-reported access to health information.
AI.conclusion: OrthoPilot represents a shift in clinical AI from predicting isolated events toward executing longitudinal management across complete care pathways. It demonstrates that AI can effectively integrate fragmented clinical data with medical knowledge to improve musculoskeletal care efficiency and outcomes.

title: Continual Learning with Elastic Regularization and Synthetic Replay for Federated MLLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Federated fine-tuning of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across distributed networks enables privacy-sensitive adaptation to evolving data streams, yet a fundamental obstacle prevents robust deployment in dynamic environments: catastrophic forgetting, wherein sequential task updates erase previously acquired knowledge across visual, linguistic, and cross-modal representations. Addressing this challenge is especially critical for autonomous networked AI operating in safety-sensitive domains, such as content moderation, where reliable retention of prior knowledge underpins system integrity. To overcome this, we propose Federated Continual Multimodal Learning (FedCMM), a framework that embeds continual-learning safeguards into the federated optimization loop at three complementary levels. At the parameter level, modality-aware elastic weight consolidation computes separate Fisher information matrices for the vision encoder, language backbone, and cross-modal projector, providing granular, asymmetry-aware protection against modality-specific forgetting. At the data level, each client trains a lightweight local generative replay module to synthesize raw-data-free embedding-level multimodal replay tuples without any raw data sharing. At the aggregation level, Task-similarity-aware gradient aggregation autonomously filters and reweights client updates by gradient cosine similarity, suppressing conflicting directions and stabilizing the global learning trajectory. Extensive experiments on two benchmarks demonstrate that FedCMM consistently outperforms recent baselines on accuracy and backward transfer, confirming that holistic, modality-aware optimization enables robust evolutive adaptation across heterogeneous networked AI deployments.

AI.tldr: FedCMM is a federated continual learning framework for multimodal LLMs that prevents catastrophic forgetting across modalities through parameter, data, and aggregation-level safeguards.
AI.motivation: Federated fine-tuning of multimodal LLMs faces catastrophic forgetting in dynamic environments, which is critical for safety-sensitive applications like content moderation where reliable knowledge retention is essential.
AI.method: Three complementary approaches: 1) Modality-aware elastic weight consolidation with separate Fisher matrices for vision, language, and cross-modal components; 2) Lightweight local generative replay modules for raw-data-free embedding-level multimodal replay; 3) Task-similarity-aware gradient aggregation that filters and reweights client updates based on gradient cosine similarity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two benchmarks show FedCMM consistently outperforms recent baselines on accuracy and backward transfer metrics.
AI.conclusion: Holistic, modality-aware optimization enables robust evolutive adaptation across heterogeneous networked AI deployments by effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting in federated multimodal learning scenarios.

title: Vertical Standardisation for High-Risk AI Systems under the EU AI Act: A Domain-Specific Framework for Algorithmic Hiring
summary: According to the recent European legislation, high-risk AI systems will have to adapt in order to comply with requirements related to specific areas, like risk management, data quality and governance, logging and traceability, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy, as outlined in the European Artificial Intelligence (AI) Act. As the standardisation process for AI is expected to remain iterative and, so far, there are no European standards on AI fully covering the challenges of algorithmic hiring, we propose specific standardisation-oriented recommendations related to the relevant AI areas specified by the European Commission. For each of these areas, we set the context by describing the requirements that AI systems in high-risk domains, and especially in recruitment, should fulfil, as well as the activities that should be carried out to ensure their appropriate use and desired performance, in line with the requirements deriving from the AI Act. Unlike existing horizontal approaches to AI governance and standardisation, this paper contributes a vertical, domain-specific framework for algorithmic hiring, and especially ranking-based recruitment systems, by mapping the requirements of the AI Act to concrete standardisation recommendations, focusing on lifecycle discrimination risks, fairness-aware data governance, explainability, human oversight, and post-deployment monitoring in recruitment systems. Even though our recommendations were informed by the outcomes of the European project FINDHR, they are not tied to the project's technical artefacts and could be implemented using alternative methods, tools, or governance mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Proposes domain-specific standardization recommendations for algorithmic hiring systems to comply with EU AI Act requirements, focusing on recruitment-specific challenges like discrimination risks, data governance, and human oversight.
AI.motivation: The EU AI Act mandates high-risk AI systems to comply with various requirements, but there are currently no European standards specifically addressing the challenges of algorithmic hiring. Existing AI governance approaches are too horizontal/general, lacking domain-specific guidance for recruitment systems.
AI.method: Proposes a vertical, domain-specific framework for algorithmic hiring by mapping AI Act requirements to concrete standardization recommendations. The approach focuses specifically on ranking-based recruitment systems and addresses lifecycle discrimination risks, fairness-aware data governance, explainability, human oversight, and post-deployment monitoring.
AI.result: Specific standardisation-oriented recommendations for algorithmic hiring that align with EU AI Act requirements. These recommendations are informed by the European FINDHR project but are implementation-agnostic and can be applied using various methods, tools, or governance mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: A domain-specific approach to AI governance and standardization is needed for algorithmic hiring, particularly to address unique challenges in recruitment systems. The proposed framework provides concrete guidance that can help recruitment systems comply with the EU AI Act while addressing specific issues like discrimination and fairness in hiring.

title: An Agentic AI Scientific Community for Automated Neural Operator Discovery
summary: We present an agentic approach to autonomous neural operator discovery based on an AI scientific community, which consists of a swarm of virtual laboratories that interact under a citation-based economy of influence. Highly-cited labs found new labs that follow their research direction and replace non-performing labs. Each virtual lab contains three agents: an LLM planner that proposes an architecture, a numerical worker that trains and measures it, and an LLM reviewer that participates in cross-lab peer review. All labs share a common vocabulary consisting of DeepONet (branch-trunk), Fourier, Transformer (attention), wavelet, and residual convolutional neural operator building blocks. We evaluate the neural operator AI scientific community on five problems, namely piecewise regression, the linear advection and Burgers 1D PDEs, and the Navier-Stokes and Darcy flow 2D PDEs, while repeating the simulation three times for each problem. The results show that the neural operator AI scientific community is capable of discovering high-accuracy, low-parameter-count neural operator architectures. All 9,623 LLM calls are logged and audited, which reveals that the virtual lab LLM planners choose to hybridize in 99.8% of their logged decisions, consistently returning multi-family hybrids. Moreover, we conducted an ablation study by replacing the LLM agents in each lab by rule-based alternatives, which caused the scientific community to collapse to non-hybridized single-family stacks in several cases, showing that LLM agency is needed to preserve diversity. The results suggest a no-free-lunch theorem for neural operators: there is no universal winner. The code, configurations, and the complete LLM transcripts are released at https://github.com/luislootx/AI-SC.

AI.tldr: An AI scientific community of virtual labs with LLM agents discovers hybrid neural operator architectures through a citation-based economy, outperforming rule-based approaches by maintaining architectural diversity across multiple PDE problems.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to automate neural operator discovery by creating a self-organizing AI scientific community that mimics human scientific processes, moving beyond hand-designed architectures or simple automated search methods.
AI.method: Uses a swarm of virtual laboratories operating under a citation-based economy. Each lab has three agents: LLM planner (proposes architecture), numerical worker (trains/evaluates), and LLM reviewer (peer review). Labs share building blocks (DeepONet, Fourier, Transformer, wavelet, residual CNN) and compete/influence through citations.
AI.result: The system discovered high-accuracy, low-parameter neural operators across 5 PDE problems. LLM planners chose hybridization in 99.8% of decisions, creating multi-family hybrids. Ablation studies showed rule-based alternatives collapsed to single-family stacks, demonstrating LLM agency is crucial for maintaining diversity.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully automates neural operator discovery through an emergent AI scientific community. Results suggest a "no-free-lunch theorem" for neural operators - no single architecture family works best universally, and LLM-driven hybridization is key to architectural diversity and performance.

title: Agentic Service-Oriented Computing: A Manifesto for the Next Frontier of Service-Oriented Computing
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Web Services (ICWS); Corresponding author: Prof. Amin Beheshti; DOI 10.1109/ICWS72778.2026.00163
summary: The rapid emergence of LLM-powered autonomous and semi-autonomous agents is reshaping software systems from static, request-response components into goal-directed, adaptive, and tool-using computational actors. As these agents move from isolated cognitive prototypes into complex distributed workflows, they confront challenges that the Service-Oriented Computing community has studied for more than two decades: composition, interoperability, quality of service, lifecycle management, governance, security, and trust. Yet much of today's agentic AI ecosystem is developing these foundations ad hoc, without the engineering rigour required for dependable enterprise and societal deployment. This paper introduces Agentic Service-Oriented Computing (ASOC) as a new research and practice area concerned with engineering agents as services, orchestrating services through autonomous and semi-autonomous agents, and governing ecosystems of agents and services under constraints of trust, cybersecurity, compliance, performance, and accountability. We articulate six foundational principles of ASOC (harness-ability, composability, lifecycle engineering, trustworthiness by design, goal-driven orchestration, and observability/accountability) and organise a five-dimensional research agenda spanning: (i) agentic services foundations and lifecycle engineering; (ii) composition, orchestration, and interoperability; (iii) governance, observability, and accountability; (iv) security, trust, and risk management; and (v) evaluation, certification, and Agentic QoS. We argue that the Services Computing community is especially well positioned to provide the conceptual and engineering spine for this emerging field, transforming agentic AI from fragmented demonstrations into dependable, service-based systems worthy of human and organisational trust.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Agentic Service-Oriented Computing (ASOC) as a new discipline that applies service-oriented computing principles to LLM-powered autonomous agents to address engineering challenges for dependable deployment.
AI.motivation: LLM-powered agents are moving from isolated prototypes to complex distributed workflows but face similar challenges that service-oriented computing has studied for decades. Current agentic AI development lacks the engineering rigor needed for enterprise deployment, developing foundations ad hoc rather than systematically.
AI.method: The paper proposes Agentic Service-Oriented Computing (ASOC) as a new research area, articulating six foundational principles and organizing a five-dimensional research agenda that brings service-oriented computing concepts to agent engineering.
AI.result: Introduction of ASOC framework with six principles (harness-ability, composability, lifecycle engineering, trustworthiness by design, goal-driven orchestration, observability/accountability) and five research dimensions covering foundations, composition, governance, security, and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The Services Computing community is well-positioned to provide the conceptual and engineering framework to transform fragmented agentic AI demonstrations into dependable, service-based systems worthy of human and organizational trust.

title: From Geometric Recovery to Causal Validation: A Reproducible Audit of Sparse Autoencoder Features, from Superposition Geometry to Causal Inertness
comment: 21 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables. Code and reproduction pipeline: https://github.com/mohamed-bal/sae-causal-audit
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are the standard for decomposing superposed neural representations into interpretable features, and evaluation relies predominantly on correlational recovery metrics -- cosine similarity between ground-truth directions and decoder atoms. We show this conflates two distinct claims: decoder-geometry alignment and encoder-activation behavior. We reproduce the superposition phase diagram of Elhage et al. (2022), identifying a convergence artifact at high sparsity and an under-described diffuse sharing regime at extreme overcompleteness. We reproduce the TopK-versus-L1 comparison of Gao et al. (2024), with direct evidence of L1 shrinkage. Our central result is causal: subjecting every recovered feature to ablation and steering, we find up to 77% of features passing a recovery bar (cosine >= 0.90) in a degraded SAE -- and 9% in a well-trained one -- are causally inert: the matched atom never fires when the feature is present, including matches at cosine ~1.000. We package the method as sae-causal-audit, a model-agnostic instrument with a deterministic pipeline. Re-auditing refines the finding: inertness decomposes by cause into structural inertness (antipodal-pair geometry, present in good SAEs) and competitive inertness (a TopK pathology of degraded SAEs), and by direction into read- and write-inertness, which five antipodal pairs dissociate completely -- unmonitorable yet steerable through the same atom, with steering specificities of 143-310 attached to zero ablation effects. We document why byte-exact reproducibility is unavailable by construction, and propose reporting it as a stack of claims with explicit scopes. Applying the instrument to a production SAE reproduces the pattern at small scale (14% inert) and surfaces an atom-collision signal: a handful of atoms recur as the nearest match for dozens of unrelated concepts, replicated across three batches.

AI.tldr: SAE evaluation relying on cosine similarity conflates geometric alignment with causal behavior. A causal audit method reveals that many features passing recovery metrics are actually causally inert, with distinct types of inertness identified.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of sparse autoencoders relies heavily on correlational metrics like cosine similarity between ground-truth directions and decoder atoms, which conflates decoder-geometry alignment with actual encoder-activation behavior. This creates a gap between what metrics suggest and what features actually do causally.
AI.method: Developed a causal audit method called 'sae-causal-audit' that subjects every recovered feature to both ablation (to test if removing it affects model behavior) and steering (to test if activating it changes model behavior). Also analyzed structural properties of SAEs by reproducing the superposition phase diagram and comparing different sparsity methods (TopK vs L1).
AI.result: Found that up to 77% of features passing recovery criteria (cosine >= 0.90) in degraded SAEs are causally inert, including 9% in well-trained ones. Identified two types of inertness: structural inertness (related to antipodal-pair geometry in good SAEs) and competitive inertness (a TopK pathology in degraded SAEs). Also discovered 'atom-collision signal' where a few atoms match dozens of unrelated concepts.
AI.conclusion: Correlational metrics for SAE evaluation are insufficient and can be misleading. A causal audit approach reveals that many apparently recovered features are actually inert. The paper calls for more rigorous evaluation methods and proposes reporting results as a stack of claims with explicit scopes rather than relying on unreliable metrics like cosine similarity.

title: Atomic Units of X: The Compression Layer of Intelligence
summary: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for understanding intelligence as a process of atomic compression and compositional reuse. We argue that cognitive, biological, computational, and organizational systems achieve scalable intelligence by decomposing complex phenomena into reusable atomic units that can be recombined into higher-order structures. Drawing on evidence from cognitive science, information theory, evolutionary biology, software engineering, medicine, legal reasoning, education, music, and artificial intelligence, the paper develops the concept of atomic units as fundamental compression layers that support efficiency, transfer, interpretability, and evolvability. The central contribution is the Compression Calculus, a formal framework for comparing surface-level representations with atomic representations and for describing how compression gains compound across abstraction layers. We introduce the Compounding Cascade thesis, according to which each additional layer of abstraction multiplicatively increases representational efficiency rather than merely adding incremental savings. The paper further argues that contemporary AI systems often operate at suboptimal levels of representation, relying on token-level processing or document-level retrieval rather than stable, concept-level atomic structures. In this view, large language models are best understood not as complete knowledge architectures, but as dynamic fusion engines capable of navigating, sequencing, and recombining atomic units. The framework provides a foundation for designing self-evolving knowledge systems that can discover, refine, and compose new primitives over time. By reframing intelligence as compression through compositional abstraction, the paper offers a unifying perspective on expertise, knowledge representation, explainable AI, and the future architecture of adaptive intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: Intelligence arises from atomic compression and compositional reuse - decomposing complex phenomena into reusable atomic units that can be recombined into higher-order structures for scalable efficiency.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified theoretical framework explaining how diverse intelligent systems (cognitive, biological, computational, organizational) achieve scalability through compression and composition, and to address limitations in current AI representations.
AI.method: Proposes Compression Calculus - a formal framework comparing surface-level vs atomic representations, and the Compounding Cascade thesis about multiplicative efficiency gains across abstraction layers. Draws evidence from multiple fields including cognitive science, information theory, evolutionary biology, software engineering, medicine, law, education, music, and AI.
AI.result: Provides a unifying perspective on intelligence as compression through compositional abstraction, explaining how atomic units enable efficiency, transfer, interpretability, and evolvability. Current AI systems operate at suboptimal representation levels, with LLMs seen as dynamic fusion engines rather than complete knowledge architectures.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers foundation for designing self-evolving knowledge systems that can discover, refine, and compose new primitives, reframing intelligence as compression through compositional abstraction to unify expertise, knowledge representation, explainable AI, and future adaptive systems.

title: Forgetful Attention: A Trainable Support-Vector Memory with Certified Selection and Exact Unlearning
summary: Attention can be viewed as an online learner over context, yet existing test-time memories cannot certify that dropping a token leaves outputs unchanged or delete its influence outright. We introduce Support Vector Attention (SV-Attention), a max-margin memory whose weights are support coefficients of a one-class SVM with fixed box parameter C. Its active-set partition gives reserve tokens exactly zero weight, certifying output-preserving eviction; a reversible incremental solver deletes a token to recover the state produced by retraining without it under the same C. In fp64 experiments, decrement and refit recover identical partitions whenever the optimum is unique, and their decision functions match to a median deviation of about 10^-9 (10^-13 on learned keys); the 10^-2 worst case is confined to ill-conditioned duplicates and remains below coefficient decay in every regime. The exact path reuses the maintained KKT inverse in a custom backward. Training uses a separate stabilized batched approximation and does not carry the exact-deletion certificate; it reaches 9,125 tokens/s on a 3.22M-parameter model, while remaining 35.8 times slower than an MPS softmax reference. At matched budgets, certified selection reaches 0.86 vs. 0.32 rare-item recall and retains 0.80 vs. 0.05 deterioration hours on real MIMIC-IV streams. We also demonstrate surgical forgetting, exact editing, patient-record deletion, and a forgettable retrieval memory over real sentence embeddings. On enwik8, the hybrid obtains 2.178 BPC vs. 2.383 for a matched-state sliding-window Transformer across seven seeds (8.6% paired improvement, p=0.001); a three-seed TinyStories result is directionally positive but not significant (p=0.057).

AI.tldr: SV-Attention replaces softmax with support vector memory for certifiable token deletion and exact influence removal, enabling certified output-preserving eviction while matching or improving language modeling performance.
AI.motivation: Existing attention mechanisms lack formal guarantees for token deletion - you can't certify that dropping a token won't change outputs or completely delete its influence from the model.
AI.method: Introduces Support Vector Attention (SV-Attention), a max-margin memory based on one-class SVM with fixed C parameter. Uses active-set partition to identify reserve tokens with zero weight (certifying eviction), and reversible incremental solver to delete tokens while recovering the exact state that retraining would produce.
AI.result: Experimental validation shows: 1) Decrement and refit recover identical partitions with median deviation ~10^-9; 2) Certified selection achieves 0.86 vs 0.32 rare-item recall and retains 0.80 vs 0.05 deterioration hours; 3) Hybrid model gets 2.178 BPC vs 2.383 for sliding-window Transformer on enwik8 (8.6% improvement); 4) Enables surgical forgetting, exact editing, patient-record deletion, and forgettable retrieval memory.
AI.conclusion: SV-Attention provides a principled, certifiable approach to token deletion in attention mechanisms, offering formal guarantees for output preservation and complete influence removal while maintaining competitive performance on language modeling tasks.

title: A Learning-Rate-Gated Failure of GRPO in a Small Language and Vision-Language Model Web Agent: A Controlled Null and Its Mechanism
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) in particular, is now run routinely on a supervised checkpoint in the hope of producing a stronger agent. We ask whether it adds skill to a small language and vision-language model web agent at the 4B to 8B scale, or whether it mostly reshapes behavior the supervised model already has. Across a control grid of 18 runs that varies learning rate, KL weight, seed, initialization, and clipping, no configuration credibly improves the success rate of a strong supervised baseline on tasks the agent has largely mastered. On the text track, moderate to high learning rates make it credibly worse. The null holds under paired testing, 25 evaluation seeds, 6 training seeds, changes to the recipe, both text and Set-of-Marks screenshot observations, and scaling the backbone to 8B; the credible harm is a text-track finding and is only nominal under Set-of-Marks. To show that the null reflects the setting and not a broken pipeline, we run the identical harness, reward, and recipe on tasks whose reward is reachable by sampling, and there the success rate rises by 22 points with a paired interval that excludes zero. GRPO therefore helps only when there is headroom to climb, meaning the sampled policy already succeeds more often than the greedy one. We then explain the failure. A middle learning rate degrades the agent and a high one collapses it, and the two regimes form a double dissociation: grafting localizes the degrade regime to the attention and MLP blocks, while the collapse regime cannot be traced to any single group, and the embedding change that dominates the weight movement is causally inert. At 4B, effective rank in the late layers tracks capability in both directions; at 8B the two come apart. This coupling is specific to the smaller model, so we report it as scale-dependent.

AI.tldr: GRPO reinforcement learning doesn't improve strong supervised baselines for 4B-8B language/vision-language web agents on mastered tasks, and can even harm performance, but does help when there's headroom for improvement.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) adds new skills or merely reshapes existing behaviors in small language and vision-language models (4B-8B scale) that are already performing well on web agent tasks.
AI.method: Conducted a control grid of 18 runs varying learning rate, KL weight, seed, initialization, and clipping. Used paired testing with 25 evaluation seeds, 6 training seeds, both text and Set-of-Marks screenshot observations, and scaled backbones to 8B. Used identical harness on tasks with reachable rewards as a positive control.
AI.result: No GRPO configuration improved success rates over strong supervised baselines on mastered tasks. Moderate to high learning rates made performance credibly worse on text tracks. When reward was reachable by sampling, success rate increased by 22 points. Found double dissociation in learning rates: middle rates degrade performance (localized to attention/MLP blocks), high rates cause collapse (not traceable to single group). Effective rank in late layers tracks capability at 4B but not at 8B.
AI.conclusion: GRPO only helps when there's headroom for improvement (when sampled policy already succeeds more often than greedy policy). The null result reflects the setting rather than a broken pipeline. The relationship between effective rank and capability is scale-dependent, working only for smaller models.

title: Speculate with Memory: Lossless Acceleration for LLM Agents
summary: Speculative execution accelerates LLM agents by using a smaller, cheaper model to predict and pre-launch the next step while the environment is idle. However, existing speculators are stateless and discard all information between tasks, preventing prediction quality from improving with experience. We equip the speculator with three online memory systems that learn from past agent trajectories: a contrastive transition table tracking action-sequence statistics, an episodic memory retrieving contextually similar segments, and a confusion tracker suppressing recurring errors. We evaluate this approach on six benchmarks spanning three speculation types: action prediction, observation prediction, and chained prediction. Memory-augmented speculation yields a 19--39\% relative accuracy improvement on action prediction and up to a $2.5\times$ increase on observation prediction tasks with repetitive action spaces. These gains grow continuously as memory accumulates and generalize across speculator models of varying cost. All speculation is lossless because it runs during idle time at zero added wall-clock cost, and the actor's trajectory is identical to non-speculative execution.

AI.tldr: Memory-augmented speculative execution improves LLM agent performance by 19-39% on action prediction and up to 2.5x on observation prediction, using three online memory systems to learn from past trajectories without added wall-clock cost.
AI.motivation: Existing speculative execution methods for LLM agents are stateless and discard information between tasks, preventing prediction quality from improving with experience over time.
AI.method: Three online memory systems are added to the speculator: (1) contrastive transition table tracking action-sequence statistics, (2) episodic memory retrieving contextually similar segments, and (3) confusion tracker suppressing recurring errors.
AI.result: Memory-augmented speculation yields 19-39% relative accuracy improvement on action prediction, up to 2.5x increase on observation prediction tasks with repetitive action spaces, with gains growing continuously as memory accumulates.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables speculation quality to improve with experience while remaining lossless (identical to non-speculative execution) and requiring zero added wall-clock cost since it runs during idle time.

title: Cluster-Weighted EDMD
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Scientific Computing and Machine Learning 2026 (SCML2026)
summary: Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) approximates Koopman operators from data, but a single global operator is inefficient when different state-space regions exhibit distinct local dynamics. We introduce Cluster-Weighted EDMD (CW-EDMD), which jointly learns a soft phase-space partition and a per-cluster EDMD operator. Its Expectation-Maximization (EM) objective assigns each transition based on both geometric proximity and prediction residuals, so clusters specialize where local Koopman models are accurate rather than where the data are dense. On Lorenz, damped pendulum, and Duffing systems, across 36 configurations and 10 seeds, CW-EDMD improves matched-degree EDMD in one-step and 5s-rollout prediction. Across 288 paired comparisons, there are significant error reductions in 258 cases, increases in 4, and no differences in 26. Median one-step error reductions are 57x, 2.7x, and 12x on pendulum, Duffing, and Lorenz, respectively.

AI.tldr: CW-EDMD improves Koopman operator learning by partitioning state-space and learning specialized local models per cluster, outperforming global EDMD on benchmark dynamical systems.
AI.motivation: Standard EDMD approximates a single global Koopman operator, which is inefficient when different state-space regions exhibit distinct local dynamics. A single operator cannot capture varying dynamical behaviors across the state space.
AI.method: Cluster-Weighted EDMD (CW-EDMD) jointly learns a soft phase-space partition and per-cluster EDMD operators using an Expectation-Maximization (EM) framework. The EM objective assigns transitions based on both geometric proximity and prediction residuals, allowing clusters to specialize where local Koopman models are accurate rather than where data are dense.
AI.result: On Lorenz, damped pendulum, and Duffing systems across 36 configurations and 10 seeds, CW-EDMD outperforms matched-degree EDMD in both one-step and 5-second rollout predictions. Across 288 paired comparisons: 258 cases show significant error reductions, 4 show increases, and 26 show no difference. Median one-step error reductions: 57x on pendulum, 2.7x on Duffing, and 12x on Lorenz systems.
AI.conclusion: CW-EDMD successfully addresses the limitation of global EDMD by adaptively partitioning state-space and learning specialized local models, demonstrating substantial improvements in prediction accuracy across benchmark dynamical systems.

title: MaxSAT-Based Feedback for Guiding Vision-Language Models in Sudoku
comment: Accepted at the 25th EPIA Conference on Artificial Intelligence, EPIA 2026. 16 pages, 1 figure, and 1 table
summary: Vision--Language Models (VLMs) have recently demonstrated promising performance on structured visual reasoning tasks, including grid-based puzzles. However, despite strong perceptual capabilities, these models lack explicit mechanisms for enforcing logical consistency and frequently generate assignments that violate underlying constraints. In this paper, we propose a neuro-symbolic approach that integrates formal constraint reasoning into the VLM solving process via a Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) oracle. Rather than computing solutions directly, the symbolic component acts as a consistency validator and refinement engine. Candidate placements generated by the VLM are encoded as soft clauses in a partial MaxSAT formulation, while Sudoku constraints remain hard clauses. When inconsistencies arise, the MaxSAT solver identifies a largest mutually consistent subset of assignments, which is then translated into structured textual and visual feedback to guide subsequent refinements. We evaluate our approach on a Sudoku dataset across multiple open-source and closed-access VLMs. Results show that MaxSAT-based feedback improves logical consistency and increases the number of solved instances, particularly in full-board refinement mode. These findings demonstrate that symbolic optimisation can enhance the reliability of vision-language reasoning.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic approach combining VLMs with MaxSAT reasoning improves Sudoku solving by using symbolic constraints to validate and refine visual-language model outputs, enhancing logical consistency.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) show strong perceptual capabilities on structured reasoning tasks like grid puzzles but lack mechanisms for enforcing logical consistency, often generating solutions that violate underlying constraints.
AI.method: Integrates formal constraint reasoning via Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) oracle. Candidate placements from VLM are encoded as soft clauses in partial MaxSAT, while Sudoku constraints remain hard clauses. When inconsistencies arise, MaxSAT solver identifies largest mutually consistent subset, which is translated into structured textual/visual feedback for refinement.
AI.result: MaxSAT-based feedback improves logical consistency and increases number of solved Sudoku instances, particularly in full-board refinement mode. Demonstrated across multiple open-source and closed-access VLMs.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic optimization through MaxSAT reasoning can enhance reliability of vision-language reasoning by providing constraint-based validation and refinement, bridging the gap between perceptual capabilities and logical consistency.

title: Proximity Features: Privacy-Compliant Cold-Start Personalization at Airbnb
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for poster presentation at the KDD 2026 Workshop on Two-sided Marketplace Optimization (TSMO 2026)
summary:
Personalization in two-sided marketplaces relies heavily on user-level features, yet for platforms with infrequent, high-consideration purchases, a large fraction of users lack sufficient history for effective recommendation, spanning both paid and organic channels. At Airbnb, a substantial share of search requests comes from logged-out or first-time users, with this challenge especially pronounced on paid-channel landing pages, leaving traditional user-level features unavailable for a large fraction of traffic. Privacy regulations and increasing restrictions on third-party cookies further limit identifier-based tracking for non-essential use cases. This paper introduces Proximity Features, a privacy-compliant feature system that groups users by geographic proximity using geo-IP data and an adaptive clustering algorithm, producing aggregated user-level signals for groups of approximately 1,000 nearby users without requiring a persistent individual identifier at inference time. Privacy is preserved by design: the pipeline operates on consented, aggregated data only within consent-gated privacy controls.
  The system is deployed in production at Airbnb, serving multiple surfaces including marketing landing pages and destination recommendation, with engagement emails integration under way. Online A/B experiments demonstrate statistically significant lifts in bookings, with the largest gains observed among users with absent or stale history.

AI.tldr: Airbnb developed Proximity Features - a privacy-compliant feature system that groups users by geographic proximity using geo-IP data and adaptive clustering to create aggregated user-level signals for ~1,000 nearby users, addressing the cold-start problem for users without sufficient history.
AI.motivation: A large fraction of Airbnb users lack sufficient history for effective recommendation due to infrequent, high-consideration purchases. Many search requests come from logged-out or first-time users, especially on paid-channel landing pages, making traditional user-level features unavailable. Privacy regulations and third-party cookie restrictions further limit identifier-based tracking for non-essential use cases.
AI.method: Proximity Features system groups users by geographic proximity using geo-IP data and an adaptive clustering algorithm. It produces aggregated user-level signals for groups of approximately 1,000 nearby users without requiring persistent individual identifiers at inference time. Privacy is preserved by design through operating on consented, aggregated data within consent-gated privacy controls.
AI.result: The system is deployed in production at Airbnb, serving multiple surfaces including marketing landing pages and destination recommendation, with engagement emails integration underway. Online A/B experiments demonstrate statistically significant lifts in bookings, with the largest gains observed among users with absent or stale history.
AI.conclusion: Proximity Features provide an effective, privacy-compliant solution to the cold-start problem in two-sided marketplaces like Airbnb. By grouping users by geographic proximity rather than relying on individual identifiers, the system enables personalization even for users without sufficient history while respecting privacy regulations and technical limitations.

title: LLMs Can See the Smoke but not the Fire: Evaluating Abductive Reasoning with Elenchos
summary: Large language models (LLMs) excel at pattern recognition and text generation, but their capacity for abductive inference - inferring latent hypotheses that explain observed behavior - remains poorly understood. Here, we introduce Elenchos (named after the Socratic method of cross-examination), a generative evaluation framework that measures abductive reasoning as a structural inverse problem. Given a reference formal system, such as the lambda-calculus, and a potentially mutated counterpart, agents must determine whether a mutation has occurred and infer the rule modifications responsible for the resulting behavioral differences. Evaluating frontier and mid-tier LLMs reveals a consistent detection-attribution dissociation: models often recognize that a system has been altered but struggle to identify the latent mutations causing the observed discrepancies. Performance degrades substantially under interacting mutations, where models frequently recover only a subset of the underlying mutations. Preliminary evidence also suggests diminishing returns from increased inference-time reasoning, with only modest improvements under larger reasoning budgets, though this finding requires further validation.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with abductive reasoning to identify latent mutations in formal systems, showing detection-attribution dissociation where they recognize changes but can't pinpoint causes.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong at pattern recognition but their capacity for abductive inference (inferring latent hypotheses from observed behavior) remains poorly understood, particularly for identifying rule modifications in formal systems.
AI.method: Introduces Elenchos, a generative evaluation framework that treats abductive reasoning as a structural inverse problem. Tests LLMs on determining whether mutations occurred in formal systems (like lambda-calculus) and inferring the rule modifications causing behavioral differences.
AI.result: LLMs show consistent detection-attribution dissociation: they often detect system alterations but struggle to identify the latent mutations causing the discrepancies. Performance degrades with interacting mutations, where models frequently recover only subsets of mutations. Preliminary evidence suggests diminishing returns from increased inference-time reasoning.
