title: Energy-Based Physics-Informed Form Finding for Clustered Tensegrity Structures
summary: Tensegrity form-finding and physical property prediction are fundamental inverse problems in structural mechanics, which aim to determine equilibrium configurations and internal force distributions. These problems are challenging due to strong nonlinearity arising from the coupling between geometry and forces, the need to ensure structural stability, and the enforcement of constraints such as boundary conditions and symmetry. Moreover, traditional methods often lack robustness to noise and outliers. This paper proposes an energy-based learning framework for clustered tensegrity form finding and physical property prediction. The proposed approach incorporates total potential energy minimization and constitutive relations into the training objective, enabling the simultaneous prediction of equilibrium nodal configurations and associated physical quantities, including member forces and force densities. By incorporating energy-based physical losses directly into the learning process, the framework improves physical consistency, robustness, and data efficiency. Numerical experiments on tensegrity structures, including prism and lander systems, show the great potential of the proposed approach and demonstrate its capability for scalable form finding and accurate prediction of structural properties.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an energy-based learning framework for tensegrity form-finding and physical property prediction that improves physical consistency, robustness, and data efficiency.
AI.motivation: Tensegrity form-finding and physical property prediction are challenging inverse problems in structural mechanics due to strong nonlinearity from geometry-force coupling, structural stability requirements, and constraints like boundary conditions and symmetry. Traditional methods lack robustness to noise and outliers.
AI.method: An energy-based learning framework that incorporates total potential energy minimization and constitutive relations into the training objective. The approach enables simultaneous prediction of equilibrium nodal configurations and physical quantities (member forces and force densities) by incorporating energy-based physical losses directly into the learning process.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on tensegrity structures (prism and lander systems) show great potential of the proposed approach, demonstrating its capability for scalable form finding and accurate prediction of structural properties.
AI.conclusion: The energy-based learning framework successfully addresses the challenges of tensegrity form-finding and physical property prediction by improving physical consistency, robustness to noise/outliers, and data efficiency while enabling simultaneous prediction of equilibrium configurations and associated physical quantities.

title: Contrastive-Collapsed Loss for Flexible and Geometrically Optimal Embeddings and Faster Convergence
summary: In this work, we introduce CoCo, a loss function aimed at learning normalized and well-structured representations. The proposed loss encourages intra-class collapse and inter-class contrast while preserving sufficient flexibility for neural networks to approximate geometrically optimal embeddings with large angular separation between classes. We provide a theoretical analysis positioning CoCo with respect to related objectives such as dot regression and cross-entropy, showing that the new proposed loss benefits from closer initialization to the optimal configuration, more informative gradients, and stronger incentives for class-wise representation collapse. Extensive experiments on diverse tabular datasets from the OpenML-CC18 benchmark show that CoCo achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods, including kernel SVM, Random Forest, dot regression, and cross-entropy-based neural networks. In addition, both theoretical arguments and empirical analyses demonstrate that the proposal promotes tighter class clustering and faster convergence. These results highlight CoCo loss as an effective objective for learning discriminative representations while maintaining competitive predictive performance.

AI.tldr: CoCo is a new loss function that learns normalized, well-structured representations by encouraging intra-class collapse and inter-class contrast while maintaining geometric flexibility for optimal embeddings.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for better representation learning with loss functions that can produce well-structured embeddings - encouraging similar representations for same-class samples while maximizing separation between different classes, while also maintaining flexibility for neural networks to find optimal geometric configurations.
AI.method: The authors introduce the CoCo loss function, which explicitly encourages intra-class collapse (samples of same class become similar) and inter-class contrast (different classes become well-separated). The loss preserves flexibility for networks to approximate geometrically optimal embeddings with large angular separation. Theoretical analysis compares CoCo to related objectives like dot regression and cross-entropy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on OpenML-CC18 tabular datasets show CoCo achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods including kernel SVM, Random Forest, dot regression, and cross-entropy-based neural networks. Both theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate CoCo promotes tighter class clustering and faster convergence compared to alternatives.
AI.conclusion: CoCo loss is an effective objective for learning discriminative representations that offers competitive predictive performance while explicitly encouraging desirable geometric properties in learned embeddings, making it a promising alternative to existing loss functions for representation learning.

title: Efficient Sequential Calibration with $O(T^{2/3-ε})$ Error Bound
summary:
We study the online binary sequential calibration problem. A recent breakthrough by \citet{dagan2024breaking} overcomes the classical \(T^{2/3}\) barrier for calibration error. Building on this result, we present an efficient randomized forecaster that achieves an expected calibration error \(O(T^{2/3-\varepsilon})\) for some constant \(\varepsilon>0\).
  Our forecaster combines the \textsc{SPR-Calibration} procedure \citep{dagan2024breaking} with an outer Blackwell-style correction layer. The \textsc{SPR-Calibration} procedure controls calibration with respect to a surrogate sequence of conditional-mean estimates, while the correction layer controls the additional error incurred when these surrogates are used to approximate the true outcomes. The analysis decomposes the total calibration error into the surrogate calibration error and the residual discrepancy between the surrogate sequence and the true outcomes. The former is bounded by the \textsc{SPR-Calibration} guarantee in \citet{dagan2024breaking}, and the latter is controlled using a quadratic potential argument together with the sparsity of the \textsc{SPR-Calibration} forecaster.

AI.tldr: An efficient randomized forecaster achieves improved expected calibration error O(T^{2/3-ε}) for online binary sequential calibration, breaking the classical T^{2/3} barrier.
AI.motivation: Previous work by Dagan et al. (2024) broke the classical T^{2/3} barrier for calibration error, but there was opportunity to develop an efficient randomized algorithm that achieves even better calibration error bounds.
AI.method: Combines SPR-Calibration procedure from Dagan et al. (2024) with an outer Blackwell-style correction layer. SPR-Calibration controls calibration with respect to surrogate conditional-mean estimates, while the correction layer controls error when these surrogates approximate true outcomes. Uses analysis decomposing total calibration error into surrogate calibration error and residual discrepancy.
AI.result: Achieves expected calibration error O(T^{2/3-ε}) for some constant ε > 0, improving upon the classical T^{2/3} barrier.
AI.conclusion: Presents an efficient randomized algorithm that provides improved calibration error bounds by combining SPR-Calibration with Blackwell-style correction, controlling both surrogate calibration error and residual discrepancies through quadratic potential arguments and sparsity properties.

title: The Spectrum Is Not Enough: When Context Helps Time-Series Forecasting
summary: A growing family of indices scores how predictable a series is from its spectrum. Practitioners increasingly read these scores as answering a different question: whether \emph{adding context}, a longer lookback, a retrieval plug-in, or a pretrained model, will help. These are not the same question. The value of context is a property of the operating point, not of the series. Any index built from the power spectrum is invariant under phase randomization, whereas the beyond-second-order value that retrieval and foundation models supply is not, because a phase-randomized series is asymptotically Gaussian. We state this as an impossibility result and isolate it with surrogate pairs that fix the spectrum and the marginal by construction. We then give a label-free, configuration-level diagnostic, the coverage deficit, whose principal term measures beyond-spectrum structure as the gain of analog over linear prediction. On seven benchmarks the prediction holds: window-keyed retrieval's value collapses across surrogate pairs (ECL median $+33\%\!\to\!-35\%$, $p{<}10^{-40}$) while every spectral index stays frozen; a foundation model's value splits into a surviving second-order part and a small beyond-linear margin that collapses; a longer linear window's value survives. Leave-one-dataset-out, the structure term predicts the sign of beyond-spectrum value where the spectral indices trail it, and the reverse holds for the second-order mechanism. We introduce no new forecaster; the contribution is the distinction, a controlled comparison, and a diagnostic for the deployment decision. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SINE.

AI.tldr: Spectral indices measure series predictability but don't indicate value of context/retrieval/pretrained models; phase-randomized surrogates reveal this distinction via controlled experiments showing retrieval value collapses while spectral indices remain fixed.
AI.motivation: Practitioners increasingly misinterpret spectral predictability indices as indicators for when adding context, longer lookbacks, retrieval plug-ins, or pretrained models will help forecasting, but these are fundamentally different questions. The value of context depends on operational conditions, not just series properties.
AI.method: Use phase-randomized surrogate pairs that fix spectrum and marginal distribution by construction to isolate the issue. Introduce coverage deficit diagnostic with principal term measuring beyond-spectrum structure as gain of analog over linear prediction. Test on seven benchmarks with controlled comparisons.
AI.result: Window-keyed retrieval's value collapses across surrogate pairs (ECL median +33%→-35%, p<10^{-40}) while spectral indices stay frozen. Foundation models split into surviving second-order part and small beyond-linear margin that collapses. Longer linear windows' value survives. Structure term predicts sign of beyond-spectrum value where spectral indices fail.
AI.conclusion: The paper distinguishes between spectral predictability and value of context/retrieval/pretrained models, provides controlled comparison methodology, and offers coverage deficit diagnostic for deployment decisions. Spectral indices don't predict beyond-second-order value that retrieval/foundation models provide.

title: TerraZero: Procedural Driving Simulation for Zero-Demonstration Self-Play at Scale
comment: Technical Report from Applied Intuition Research
summary: Training robust autonomous driving agents requires a simulator that is fast enough for reinforcement learning at scale, realistic enough to ground behavior in real-world map structure, and diverse enough to cover the safety-critical long tail that logged data rarely contains. We present TerraZero, a procedural driving simulator and self-play training stack. A configurable C engine runs simulation on the CPU and policy inference on the GPU over a zero-copy path, sustaining 1.3M agent-steps per second on a single server-grade GPU, far faster than existing object-level simulators, while keeping fidelity lighter single-agent systems omit: heterogeneous agents, multiple dynamics models, and full traffic-rule enforcement. TerraZero treats logged data only as a source of real-world map geometry, populating each map with randomized rule-based road users and signal controllers and randomizing agent dynamics, rewards, and sizes per episode, so a map yields an unbounded set of scenarios. Every reported policy trains from scratch by reinforcement learning alone on a compute-efficient self-play recipe across GPUs, with zero human demonstrations and no fallback planner at inference. Policies generalize zero-shot across cities and datasets, including emergent left-hand-traffic driving without explicit supervision. As an ego policy, TerraZero is the first fully learned policy to top the InterPlan long-tail benchmark, ahead of larger learned planners; on routine-driving val14 it ranks among the best approaches and is the safest, posting the best collision and time-to-collision scores. On Waymo Open Sim Agents realism the same recipe outperforms other demonstration-free methods and is competitive with the strongest reference-anchored self-play method. One stack serves both roles: driving policies across dynamics for cars and trucks, and sim agents that jointly control vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists.

AI.tldr: TerraZero: A fast, procedural driving simulator for scalable RL training that achieves state-of-the-art performance without human demonstrations, using self-play across diverse randomized scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current simulators for autonomous driving training face a triple challenge: they need to be fast enough for large-scale reinforcement learning, realistic enough to ground behavior in real-world maps, and diverse enough to cover safety-critical long-tail scenarios that logged data rarely captures.
AI.method: Developed TerraZero, a CPU-based configurable C engine simulator with GPU policy inference via zero-copy path, achieving 1.3M agent-steps/second. Uses logged data only for map geometry, then procedurally generates scenarios with randomized rule-based agents, dynamics models, rewards, and sizes. Trains policies from scratch using compute-efficient self-play reinforcement learning across GPUs with no human demonstrations or fallback planners.
AI.result: Policies generalize zero-shot across cities and datasets (including emergent left-hand traffic driving). As ego policy: First fully learned policy to top InterPlan long-tail benchmark; on routine-driving val14 ranks among best approaches with best safety scores. On Waymo Open Sim Agents realism: Outperforms other demonstration-free methods, competitive with strongest reference-anchored self-play. Single stack supports both driving policies (cars/trucks) and sim agents (vehicles/pedestrians/cyclists).
AI.conclusion: TerraZero demonstrates that procedural simulation with self-play reinforcement learning can produce highly capable autonomous driving policies without human supervision, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks while being orders of magnitude faster than existing simulators for scalable training.

title: The Seriality Gap in Video Diffusion Models
comment: Jorge Diaz Chao and Konpat Preechakul contributed equally. 24 pages, 12 figures, and 5 tables. Project page: https://seriality-gap.jdiazchao.com
summary: When one ball strikes another, then another, video models should predict the consequences of each bounce. In controlled experiments on multi-ball hard-sphere dynamics, we find that the performance of standard bidirectional video diffusion degrades as the causal chain lengthens, even when provided more denoising steps. In a length-matched single-ball control, where ball-ball interactions are absent, the degradation largely disappears, isolating dependent-event structure rather than video length as the cause. Across intervention studies, methods that increase effective serial computation improve performance disproportionately, including autoregressive/blockwise generation and architectural depth. We identify this pattern as the seriality gap: a mismatch between tasks requiring growing serial computation and video diffusion models whose denoising loop does not provide scalable serial compute. We then prove that, for deterministic video prediction, denoising steps do not add serial computation beyond the backbone, indicating a structural obstacle for video diffusion on serial reasoning and simulation tasks.

AI.tldr: Video diffusion models struggle with multi-ball dynamics due to limited serial computation in denoising loops, creating a "seriality gap" for sequential reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to understand why video diffusion models perform poorly on tasks requiring sequential reasoning, specifically multi-ball collision dynamics where events are causally dependent.
AI.method: Conducted controlled experiments comparing standard bidirectional video diffusion on multi-ball hard-sphere dynamics versus single-ball control, analyzed performance degradation with longer causal chains, tested interventions including autoregressive/blockwise generation and architectural depth, and theoretically proved limitations of denoising steps.
AI.result: Found that standard video diffusion performance degrades as causal chain lengthens; degradation disappears in single-ball control; methods increasing serial computation improve performance disproportionately; identified "seriality gap" mismatch; proved denoising steps don't add serial computation beyond backbone.
AI.conclusion: Video diffusion models have a fundamental structural limitation for serial reasoning tasks due to insufficient serial computation in denoising loops, creating a "seriality gap" that requires architectural or methodological innovations to address.
